title: Meade County deputies keep busy with civil paper service
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: Meade County Sheriff Pat West told members of the planning commission on March 17 that calls to serve civil papers have nearly doubled from where they were at this time last year. Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Meade County deputies keep busy with civil paper service
Meade County Sheriff Pat West told members of the planning commission on March 17 that calls to serve civil papers have nearly doubled from where they were at this time last year. Courtesy photo  

STURGIS — The Meade County Sheriff’s Office has been inundated with civil service papers, and Sheriff Pat West said demands for service only keep going up. 

On March 17 West told the Meade County Commission that his officers have been spending more time than ever serving divorce papers, foreclosure notices, seizing property due to lack of payment, garnished wages notices, property sale notices, and more. Next month, he said his office will oversee four foreclosed house sales. 

“It’s just people not paying their bills and getting into trouble,” West said. 

In February, West reported his deputies made 335 civil service attempts, and successfully served papers 163 times, resulting in $9,709 in fees collected for the sheriff’s office, which gets paid for successful service attempts. That compares to last month’s 241 attempts, and 144 successful services. 

By comparison, sheriff’s deputies made 191 traffic stops and issued 71 citations in February, compared to 215 stops and 105 citations last year. 

The numbers for civil service are nearly double the totals from last year at the same time. In February 2025, the sheriff’s office made 191 attempts to serve civil papers, with 96 successful services. In January 2025, deputies made 280 attempts, and successfully served 224 civil papers.

title: Tracy Kerwin drives high-performing  fundraising as new SURF Foundation director
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Tracy Kerwin, was recently named the SURF Foundation director. Photo by Stephen Kenny/SURF
_full_text:
Tracy Kerwin drives high-performing  fundraising as new SURF Foundation director
Tracy Kerwin, was recently named the SURF Foundation director. Photo by Stephen Kenny/SURF 

LEAD — The Sanford Underground Research Facility Foundation has a new director. 

Tracy Kerwin has spent a great deal of her career giving back. 

Her first job out of college was with the United Way in Iowa, where she quickly rose through the ranks, landing herself in Florida during a rather tumultuous hurricane season. 

“United Way branches across the state were activated, along with the Red Cross and the Salvation Army, to go out and deliver aid immediately where it was needed. So that was a very solid early-career learning experience and a privilege to be a part of the recovery efforts,” Kerwin said. 

Later, after finding success in financial industry marketing, she transitioned into frontline fundraising for the American Cancer Society, where she developed her expertise in philanthropy and volunteer engagement. She went on to support and advance fundraising efforts at Cleveland Clinic, Martin Health Foundation, and The Wertheim UF Scripps Institute at the University of Florida. 

“I think one of the best and most rewarding parts of my job is seeing philanthropic gifts in action. To be able to bring a donor’s vision to life in a way that makes an actual difference for them and for the world,” Kerwin said. 

Now she heads the SURF Foundation after being named to the position in February. As the philanthropic arm of SURF, the Foundation builds relationships around the globe and opens doors for public and corporate contributions to support SURF’s mission to inspire learning and advance world-class science. 

World-class science underway at SURF has a proven track record of attracting world-class philanthropy. In leading the SURF Foundation, Kerwin will employ high-performing, fundraising strategies that engage donors with compelling narratives, demonstrated impact, and strong stewardship. 

“When you’re working for an organization like the United Way, or the American Cancer Society, a hospital, or a research institution like SURF, it’s about creating relationships with those who find meaning and value in the mission and vision,” Kerwin said. 

In this golden age of science, SURF’s mission is vital. The future holds exciting opportunities — from pushing quantum capabilities into new territory to particle physics experiments that could revolutionize our understanding of the universe. Pioneering biology research is generating advancements in medicine, carbon capture, and astrobiology, alongside the potential for game-changing geothermal energy. Beyond the experiments themselves, SURF is also focused on building the scientific community necessary to foster these breakthroughs with multiple programs led by the Institute for Underground Science at SURF. 

“The sheer ingenuity of the engineering team and the science teams who work behind the scenes every single day to make this facility run safely while enabling potentially world-changing scientific discoveries — it’s all really awe-inspiring and amazing how many really brilliant people are amassed here at SURF making this happen.” 

The SURF Foundation provides funding for science programming in the Northern Hills area and among the scientific community. Some programming includes CETUP, which brings theoretical and physical scientists together to share ideas and collaborate, Deeper Talks, which hosts engaging STEM lectures for the community, and more. 

With research that will last well into this century, the people who will lead SURF tomorrow are kids in kindergarten today. This is why half the organization’s mission is focused on education with initiatives that range from the Davis-Bahcall Scholars Program to unique STEM teacher training fellowships. 

“I think people who are deeply curious about science often have a passion for seeing STEM education take root and be very strong in the United States,” Kerwin said. “Our world is getting more and more complex every single day, and we need the best and the brightest minds out there solving those problems for us.”

At SURF, inspiring those future minds to become world-class problem solvers is, in large part, privately funded. Kerwin is enthusiastic about the possibilities.   

“My big charge is to help bring this message and garner national and eventually international resources for SURF and The Institute,” she said. “I’ve also come to realize there are still a lot of untapped resources locally in the state of South Dakota, in the Black Hills in particular.”

For Kerwin, the momentum building behind SURF’s ongoing and future experiments translates directly into opportunity. As America’s Underground Lab, SURF is home to some of the most consequential science on the planet. Kerwin’s job is to help make sure the world—and its philanthropists—know it.

title: Lawrence Co. sets price per gallon for magnesium chloride treatments
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: DEADWOOD — People living on secondary county roads in Lawrence County can now request magnesium chloride treatments for dust control after county commissioners set the price per gallon.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

DEADWOOD — People living on secondary county roads in Lawrence County can now request magnesium chloride treatments for dust control after county commissioners set the price per gallon.

County commissioners approved Highway Superintendent John Bey’s recommended price of $1.38 per gallon at the March 24 meeting. The price per gallon was set after the county awarded bids for materials at the March 17 meeting. Setting the cost per gallon for residents requesting the surface is done via resolution, a process the city adopted last year.

“Last year we actually put it into the legal form, so the public can actually request this (service) through a form,” Bey said. “It kind of keeps everything on the up and up.”

Bey said interested parties will be charged only for materials. 

“We provide the labor and the equipment,” he said. “We’re already being paid for that. We just charge them for the actual material.”

Bey said last year the county brought in approximately $13,000 in revenue from treating secondary county roads upon resident’s request.

“We don’t do anything on private property, this is all on secondary county roads,” he said. “We don’t chloride every single road, but if people want dust control in front of their homes this is our way of trying to help out with that.”

For more information about requesting chloride treatments on a county secondary road, contact the highway department at (605) 578-2183.

title: Legislature commits more money to prevent ‘catastrophic failure’ at dam near Aberdeen
byline: By Meghan O’Brien Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — A plan to replace the dam and spillway at a northeast South Dakota lake will receive $8.2 million in additional state funding, lawmakers decided Monday, raising the total funds available for the project to more than $34 million.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — A plan to replace the dam and spillway at a northeast South Dakota lake will receive $8.2 million in additional state funding, lawmakers decided Monday, raising the total funds available for the project to more than $34 million.

The state House voted 61-3 and the Senate voted 28-2 to support the spending. Because lawmakers didn’t finish work on the bill earlier this month, they dealt with it Monday on Veto Day, which is the final legislative day reserved for considering the governor’s vetoes.

The deteriorating, nearly 90-year-old Richmond Lake Dam is state-owned and the state’s responsibility to fix, said Sen. Mark Lapka, R-Leola. The lake, which is part of a state recreation area, is on a creek several miles upstream from the city of Aberdeen.

“If a catastrophic failure would occur, there is, in fact, people’s lives in danger, and it’s one of those what-ifs that you have to take seriously and address,” Lapka said. “What we have the opportunity here today to do is to leave it better than when we got it.”

The location of Richmond Lake in northeast South Dakota.

This year’s bill will transfer $8.2 million from the state’s unclaimed property trust fund to the Office of School and Public Lands for the project. Unclaimed property consists of an array of abandoned or forgotten private assets, including money from bank accounts, PayPal accounts, stocks and even the contents of safe deposit boxes. Holders of the money or items, such as banks, try to find the owners. The property reverts to the state after three years.

Office of School and Public Lands Commissioner Brock Greenfield said the state has a legal requirement to repair the dam.

“I hate that I have to be back here asking for funds, but this is a state responsibility,” Greenfield told a panel of 6 lawmakers Monday morning.

Some lawmakers were concerned with the office returning again for more funding after several prior approvals, but said the risk of damage to life and property outweighed concerns about the cost.

Republican Rep. Al Novstrup, who lives just south of the dam in Aberdeen, said if the structure breaks, the damage could be seven times that of the 1972 Black Hills flood, which caused an estimated $165 million worth of damage and also killed 238 people, primarily in Rapid City.

“We know this dam is a high-hazard dam,” he said. “And we’re going to worry about $8 million? This makes no sense at all.”

The governor signed the spending bill immediately Monday, so the Office of School and Public Lands now has a total of $34.9 million available for the project, including money from past spending bills. Greenfield told lawmakers that’s “probably a reliable number,” based on a bid from Journey Group, a Sioux Falls construction company.

Lawmakers have appropriated state money for the project several times since 2022, but Greenfield said his office received less federal funding than anticipated.

“There we were back last year, and we thought we had enough to get us by and we learned this year that we didn’t,” he said. “But I’m hopeful that with the appropriation that has now been passed, that we can get the project done, get the project done right.”

The multi-phase project will take until 2028 to complete, according to Greenfield.

title: Governor’s vetoes stand for meat ban and in-home care regulations
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — South Dakota lawmakers failed Monday to reach the two-thirds majority needed to override Gov. Larry Rhoden’s vetoes of a lab-grown meat ban and regulations on non-medical home care providers.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — South Dakota lawmakers failed Monday to reach the two-thirds majority needed to override Gov. Larry Rhoden’s vetoes of a lab-grown meat ban and regulations on non-medical home care providers.

The lab-grown meat bill would have classified the products as adulterated food, effectively banning their sale in the state. Rhoden argued in his veto message last month that South Dakota should not ban products simply because some people dislike them. He also warned of legal concerns raised by lawsuits against similar bans in other states.

Rhoden instead supported a compromise bill that imposed a 5-year moratorium on lab-grown meat in South Dakota. He signed that bill into law on March 11. 

On Monday, which was the day set aside to consider the governor’s vetoes at the Capitol in Pierre, the lab-grown meat ban died in the House of Representatives. The override vote was 32-32 with 6 members absent, which fell short of the 47-vote threshold and made Senate consideration unnecessary.

Lesterville Republican Rep. Julie Auch sponsored the vetoed bill. She told House colleagues that livestock production is a way of life in South Dakota. 

“It’s wholesome meat raised the way God intended,” Auch said. She described lab-grown meat as a threat to replace the cattle industry.

The other bill Rhoden vetoed would have required licenses for non-medical home care agencies and created penalties for violations. 

Rhoden explained his veto in a letter last week, saying the regulations in the bill are too broad and create a false sense of consumer protection because the state Department of Health doesn’t have the authority to examine or verify the background checks required in the bill. A representative with the state Department of Health opposed the bill during legislative committee hearings.

Rhoden also said he directed the department to work with stakeholders on a better framework “that provides meaningful protections without placing unnecessary burdens on those who provide essential services.” 

Brookings Republican Rep. Mellissa Heermann, the bill’s sponsor, argued Monday that Rhoden’s veto overstated the bill’s reach and ignored how much the bill had already been narrowed through talks with stakeholders and the Department of Health. 

She and others said the measure was a basic consumer-protection bill for elderly and other vulnerable people receiving non-medical care at home, requiring things like liability insurance and a background check. 

Heermann said the Department of Health could work out implementation details through the rulemaking process. 

“They have within their ability, right now, to correct any problems that they perceive,” she said. 

The House’s override vote was 36-27 with 7 members absent, falling short of the required 47 votes and ending the bill’s journey without the need for Senate consideration of the veto.

The Legislature finished the rest of its work earlier this month. The conclusion of Veto Day brings the 2026 legislative session to an end.

title: Gaming revenues show a positive first quarter in Deadwood this year
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: DEADWOOD — According to new statistics released by the South Dakota Commission on Gaming, gaming in Deadwood has continued its upward climb this year.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

DEADWOOD — According to new statistics released by the South Dakota Commission on Gaming, gaming in Deadwood has continued its upward climb this year. 

In February, the gaming handle rose by 17.03% compared to the same month last year. Slots increased by 17.41% and table games saw an increase of 13.11%, while sports wagering increased by 2.69% over the same time period. 

Overall, gaming activity is up 13.26% in the first quarter of the year compared to the same time period over last year. 

“The first quarter of 2026 started very well,” said George Milos, director of the Deadwood Gaming Association. “Outstanding weather throughout the month coupled with a lively Mardi Gras weekend attracted many visitors to Deadwood and contributed to these positive numbers. We’re optimistic that this positive momentum will continue.”

Operators gave out $1,989,094.46 in “free play” incentives to customers, bringing the total taxable revenue up to $11,409,421.58 for the month. Of that total, a total gaming tax of $1,026,847.94 was collected. The state general fund received 4114,094.22 of the collected gaming tax, while $365,101.49 was allocated to South Dakota Tourism and $91,275.37 was allocated to Lawrence County.

title: Crews collect pavement  data in Spearfish
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Pavement assessment in Spearfish was set to begin Monday night. Photo courtesy city of Spearfish
_full_text:
Crews collect pavement  data in Spearfish
Pavement assessment in Spearfish was set to begin Monday night. Photo courtesy city of Spearfish 

SPEARFISH — Beginning Monday, Infrastructure Management Services (IMS) crews started collecting pavement condition data on roads in the Spearfish city limits.

The city hired IMS to complete a city-wide pavement condition assessment, so that public works can better prioritize maintenance projects. 

IMS will slowly pull a device called a fast falling weight deflectometer,” on a small trailer behind a truck, between 6 p.m. and 6 a.m. in the evenings until the assessment is complete. 

The work is expected to take about a week, although data collection will be suspended during freezing or inclement weather. Testing does no damage road surfaces, and is not expected to be loud or disruptive.

title: Fact brief: Does SD have the longest stretch of Republican governors?
byline: By Michael Klinski South Dakota News Watch
description: Q : Does South Dakota have the longest stretch of Republican governors?
_full_text:
SDNewsWatchLogo

Q: Does South Dakota have the longest stretch of Republican governors?

A: Yes. South Dakota has the longest uninterrupted Republican hold on a governor’s office of any state in the country.

A Democratic governor has not held office in South Dakota in more than 47 years, when Harvey Wollman was the 26th governor of the state after replacing Richard Kneip, who resigned. He was replaced by Republican Bill Janklow in his first eight-year term on Jan. 1, 1979.

Utah has the second-longest run of Republican governors – a Democrat last served in 1985.

South Dakota has had three Democratic governors since the 1950s (five overall): Ralph Herseth (1959-1961), Kneip (1971-78) and Wollman (1978-79).

Only 2 Democrats have earned more than 45% of the vote since the party last held the state’s highest office. Billy Sutton received 47.6% of the vote against Republican Kristi Noem (51%), and Lars Herseth got 48.2% of the vote against George S. Mickelson (51.8%) for the first of his 2 terms.

title: Names released in fatal crash outside of Deadwood
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: DEADWOOD — The names of people involved in the 4-vehicle crash outside of Deadwood have been released by the South Dakota Department of Transportation.
_full_text:
DEADWOOD — The names of people involved in the 4-vehicle crash outside of Deadwood have been released by the South Dakota Department of Transportation. 

According to the South Dakota Department of Transportation, 92-year-old Robert Erickson from Piedmont was driving a 2019 Chevrolet Suburban, traveling eastbound on Highway 14A near mile marker 43 when he allegedly crossed the centerline and sideswiped an oncoming 2010 Ford Flex, driven by Joanna Silvia Barlow from Lead with a 2-year-old passenger. Erickson’s vehicle then struck an oncoming Ford F-150 head-on, driven by Justin Weaver and passenger Lindsey Weaver of Rapid City, that went off the road and into the ditch. Erickson’s vehicle then struck a GMC Sierra traveling westbound, driven by Cody Lynn Oliver from Deadwood. 

Erickson was pronounced deceased at the scene. Preliminary investigations show he was not wearing a seatbelt. 

Justin and Lindsey Weaver were transported to Monument Health in Rapid City. According to reports, Justin had non-life-threatening injuries and Lindsey had life-threatening injuries. Preliminary investigations show Justin was wearing a seatbelt, but it is still under investigation if Lindsey was wearing a seatbelt. 

Barlow and the 2-year-old passenger and Oliver were not injured. Preliminary investigations show all 3 were wearing seatbelts.

The South Dakota Highway Patrol is investigating the crash. 

The Pioneer will continue to report on the incident as more information becomes available.

title: Vehicle crash near Deadwood  results in 1 casualty, multiple injuries
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: One individual involved in a 4 vehicle crash outside of Deadwood was transported via Life Flight to Monument Health in Rapid City with life-threatening injuries. Photo courtesy of the Deadwood Volunteer Fire Department
_full_text:
Vehicle crash near Deadwood  results in 1 casualty, multiple injuries
One individual involved in a 4 vehicle crash outside of Deadwood was transported via Life Flight to Monument Health in Rapid City with life-threatening injuries. Photo courtesy of the Deadwood Volunteer Fire Department 

DEADWOOD — A crash involving 4 vehicles near mile marker 43 on US Highway 14A near Deadwood has resulted in one casualty and multiple injuries. 

According to the South Dakota Highway Patrol, a 92-year-old man from Piedmont driving a 2019 Chevrolet Suburban traveling east on Highway 14A crossed the centerline, sideswiping an oncoming 2010 Ford Flex. The Ford, driven by a 41-year-old female from Lead carrying a 2-year-old passenger, spun and came to rest on the centerline. 

The Suburban then struck an oncoming Ford F-150 head-on, driven by a 44-year-old male and carrying a 41-year-old passenger both from Rapid City, which went off the road and into the ditch. The Suburban then struck a 2005 GMC Sierra, driven by a 42-year-old male from Deadwood. The Suburban then came to rest in the westbound passing lane. 

The driver of the Suburban was pronounced deceased at the scene. The driver and passenger in the F-150 were transported via Life Flight to Monument Health in Rapid City with life-threatening injuries. The driver and passenger in the Ford Flex and the GMC Sierra were not injured. 

Deadwood Volunteer Fire Department Chief Joel Ellis was the first to respond to the scene in his personal vehicle. He said firefighters assisted by blocking Highway 14A further east in Boulder Canyon and at the intersection of Highway 14A and Highway 85, as well as assisting with triage, driving ambulances, and assisting medics inside the ambulance. 

“It was a mass casualty incident, but all of the agencies that were there worked very, very well together,” said Ellis. “From an incident standpoint, it went as well as it could. I think our community should be proud of the responding agencies that were out there, because it was a difficult scene.”

Responding agencies include the Deadwood Police Department, Deadwood Volunteer Fire Department, Lawrence County Sheriff’s Department, Lawrence County Emergency Management, Deadwood Monument Health Ambulance, Spearfish Ambulance, Black Hills Life Flight, the Lawrence County Coroner, and Sunshine Towing. 

The South Dakota Highway Patrol is investigating the incident. All information released so far is preliminary. 

The Pioneer will continue to report on the incident as more information is made available.

title: ‘You’re never too old to play’:  Frick’s arcade welcomes the young and young at heart for lively, nostalgic, in-person gaming
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: Game on! Husband and wife Cameron and Paula Frick have loved arcades since they first met and started dating. Now, they are the owners of a new arcade in Spearfish. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson
_full_text:
Frick's 1.JPG
Game on! Husband and wife Cameron and Paula Frick have loved arcades since they first met and started dating. Now, they are the owners of a new arcade in Spearfish. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

SPEARFISH — When husband and wife Cameron and Paula Frick were first dating, they had a favorite spot for romantic evenings out: Toomey’s Mill Arcade, which was located across from the Spearfish Police Station on Illinois Street, where Orchard Dental sees patients today.

Cameron, according to Paula, loved the flashing lights and fast footwork of the arcade’s dancing game.

That’s why, when the pair decided to take the leap from careers in elder care to open their own nostalgic business, they knew they needed one thing in particular.

Front and center at Frick’s Arcade, their new business at 208 Colorado Blvd., is a dance game.

“We’ve always been gamers, and we are kids at heart,” Cameron said.

“The dance game was the first one on the list. Because that is what he would always do at Toomey’s,” Paula added. She picked out the snowcross race game, which is her current favorite.

“We had so many good memories from Toomey’s,” Cameron said. “That was why we were like, we should open an arcade. Everyone comes together there.”

 Frick's 3.JPG
Owners Cameron and Paula Frick opened Frick’s arcade on Colorado Boulevard about 8 weeks ago. Frick said the business has been so much fun, he feels like he’s only been working there a week. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

The Fricks worked together at Edgewood Senior Living for 15 years. Opening an arcade was a dream they held in their heads and hearts for years. Then, about 8 weeks ago, they decided it was time to give it a try.

“We knew that it was going to be kind of a risk because of the technology now,” Cameron said.

The Fricks are avid gamers at home, but they saw a need for a different kind of entertainment experience. an experience that could bring people together, actively and in person — the way Toomey’s brought Cameron and Paula together. 

“We spent a lot of time picking out games. We knew that was going to be very crucial to get people coming back. A big focus of a lot of the games is that they are active. You are not just sitting there. We do have those too, but a lot of the games, you’re moving around while you’re playing,” Cameron said. “The P to P (player to player), in-person interaction, we are trying to bring that back to the community.”

 Frick's 5 alt.JPG
Patrons of Frick’s Arcade in Spearfish can trade real tickets for digital tickets, which can be exchanged for snacks and prizes. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

Now, they have 18 games — designed to appeal to audiences of all ages.

“We do have a gentleman who comes in now, and that is his gym,” Paula said. “He plays the dancing game for an hour or 2, and doesn’t even go to the gym anymore. You’re not thinking about the exercise as you’re doing it.”

The arcade itself is kid-friendly by design. Frick’s doesn’t have a bar or kitchen, so that patrons can focus on family-centered fun. There are snacks and soft drinks available to purchase if gamers work up an appetite, but Cameron said he wants the place to feel inviting for both the young and young at heart.

“Nothing warms my heart more than kids having a good time in here,” Cameron said. “I thought when we opened it was going to be a lot more of the 6 or 7 to like 15 (year-olds), but honestly, it’s been crazy the range of people we’ve had come in. We have a lot of young kids, but we have groups of adults.”

Games range from arcade classics like skee ball, air hockey, and whack-a-mole (Frick’s has a Gotham-city-inspired version), to modernized racing and shooting games. There are claw machines. There are prizes. There are a few contemporary upgrades to the traditional token-to-ticket arcade system.

“You get a play card. A play card is a dollar. It is a one-time fee, and you keep the card. Then, you can bring it back multiple times. eTickets are on the play cards. You don’t have to keep saving them up and carrying tickets around. Then you get a bonus if you get more credits. The more credits you get at one point, the more credits you get,” Cameron said.  “The tickets and the credits, they don’t expire at any point, so if you are trying to go for a big-ticket item, you don’t have to worry about the tickets going away or anything like that. If you come back in 6 months, you don’t have to worry about the credits resetting and losing your money.”

The Fricks hope people will want to come back again and again, improving their skills and enjoying their favorite games. They also hope to expand their offerings and maybe even introduce laser tag one day.

For now, the arcade is open Wednesday- Thursday from noon- 8 p.m., Fridays from noon- 10 p.m., and Saturdays from 10 a.m.- 10 p.m.

Those hours have brought in lots of families, Paula said. In a bit of a full-circle development for the Fricks, the arcade has also become popular with teens.

“A lot of teenagers come here for date night,” Paula said.

title: Oakridge Cemetery roadways set for a facelift
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: Oakridge Cemetery in Deadwood is set to receive a 2-inch overlay of asphalt on all roads and pathways. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan
_full_text:
Oakridge Cemetery roadways set for a facelift
Oakridge Cemetery in Deadwood is set to receive a 2-inch overlay of asphalt on all roads and pathways. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan 

DEADWOOD — The roads and pathways at Oakridge Cemetery in Deadwood are scheduled to receive a 2-inch overlay, improving visitor’s access and experiences at the site. 

Deadwood city commissioners awarded a $78,267.50 bid to Sacrison Paving, of Whitewood, for the project at the March 16 meeting. 

Sacrison is expected to place a 2-inch overlay of asphalt on all roads and a petromat where needed to repair cracks in the roads. 

Randy Adler said 2 other contractors looked at the project but said that the roads were too narrow with too sharp of turns for their equipment.

Adler said the road improvements are the performed on the cemetery roads for approximately 15 to 20 years. 

“It’s been repaired here and there, and it’s had some sealer put on it, but that’s the only thing that’s been done,” he said. “It’s in desperate need of an overlay. It’ll look nce when it’s done.”

The project is being paid for by the capital improvement line item.

title: Warden from Virginia hired to run newly constructed Rapid City women’s prison
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Eric Aldridge, Warden
_full_text:
Warden from Virginia hired to run newly  constructed Rapid City women’s prison
Eric Aldridge, Warden 

RAPID CITY — The South Dakota Department of Corrections hired Eric Aldridge as warden of the Rapid City women’s prison, the department announced on Friday. 

The $87 million facility, which is South Dakota’s second women’s prison, is under construction and expected to open in July.

Aldridge, most recently warden of the medium-security Fluvanna Correctional Center for Women in Troy, Virginia, will begin his job in South Dakota on April 1.

Aldridge has also worked as a rehabilitation counselor, security assessment analyst and in facility leadership positions, according to the department. Aldridge said he plans to “implement industry best practices” at the new facility.

“My focus is on positive change and improving the lives of offenders so that they will re-enter society as productive citizens,” Aldridge said in a news release.

The Rapid City prison, designed for minimum and medium-security inmates, will house up to 300 women. The facility will also house a mother-infant program for up to 12 mothers.

The prison will help ease overcrowding at an existing women’s prison in Pierre, which will continue to operate. 

The new facility will incorporate a therapeutic community model for substance use disorder treatment. That means women will receive four to five hours of intervention and support daily from medical, behavioral health and vocational professionals.

More than half of imprisoned women in the state have drug convictions, and more than 90% have substance use disorders, according to state reports.

Department of Corrections Secretary Nick Lamb said the model will be a “first-of-its-kind” program in the state.

“We have not used this model in our other facilities because of a lack of available programming space,” Lamb said in a news release. “This modern facility allows us to do so.”

Women in the prison will also have the chance to participate in a business-entrepreneurship program and earn a certificate in hospitality training.

Other positions are still being staffed, including security, case management, medical and behavioral health services.

Meanwhile, the state is preparing to build a $650 million men’s prison in northeast Sioux Falls. It will replace the oldest parts of the Sioux Falls penitentiary, which dates to 1881.

title: 4 candidates compete for Lead commission seats
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: LEAD — Four candidates will compete for 2 seats on the Lead City Commission in the next municipal election, June 2.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

LEAD — Four candidates will compete for 2 seats on the Lead City Commission in the next municipal election, June 2. 

City Commissioners Rob Carr and Al Rohrer’s terms expire this year. Rohrer, Jeremiah Frederickson, Dustin Schumacher, and Valerie Meiners have submitted petitions for the commission seats, which are both for 3-year terms. 

Earlier this month, Kayla Klein resigned from the Lead City Commission. On March 16, Mayor Dustin Heupel appointed Dory Hanson to fill that position through the end of Klein’s term, which expires next year. While Lead City Administrator Robin Lucero originally reported that the city could take the top 3 candidates to fill all the open positions, Heupel said that is not in line with state law. South Dakota Codified Law 9-13-14.2 states that the city can hold a special election to choose a city commissioner who has resigned before the end of the term, if the city has an enabling ordinance in place before the resignation occurs. The city of Lead did not have an ordinance in place to allow a special election before Klein resigned. 

Since Klein’s term was not expired, a special election would be required to fill her seat on the commission, despite the scheduled municipal election in June. 

The Lead Area Chamber of Commerce will host a candidate forum at 6 p.m., April 7, at the Historic Homestake Opera House. Residents are encouraged to send their questions ahead of time to director@leadmethere.org. Chamber Executive Director Jami Grangaard said the pre-submitted questions will be addressed first, and if there is time questions may be taken from the audience.

title: Trade mission to Japan, South Korea carried hope of diversifying soybean market beyond China
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Members of a South Dakota trade mission pose at the U.S. Embassy in Tokyo, Japan, in March 2026. Photo courtesy of the Governor’s Office
_full_text:
Trade mission to Japan, South Korea carried hope of diversifying soybean market beyond China
Members of a South Dakota trade mission pose at the U.S. Embassy in Tokyo, Japan, in March 2026. Photo courtesy of the Governor’s Office 

South Dakota officials are trying to strengthen trade relationships with Japan and South Korea as soybean farmers look to diversify beyond China, which has reduced its purchases. 

Jerry Schmitz, executive director of the South Dakota Soybean Research and Promotion Council, was part of a delegation that visited the two countries earlier this month. 

“Really, we’re laying the pathway for additional sales in the future,” he said. 

The delegation also included Lt. Gov. Tony Venhuizen, South Dakota Trade President and CEO Jesse Fonkert, Governor’s Office of Economic Development Commissioner Bill Even, and state Agriculture and Natural Resources Secretary Hunter Roberts.

Schmitz said Japan and South Korea are not new markets for South Dakota soybeans or soy-fed pork. He said the trade mission was less about signing immediate contracts and more about building trust with hog feed suppliers, grocery representatives and other buyers.

Schmitz said the long-term goal is a broader demand for soybeans and more value-added exports, such as pork raised on soybean feed, rather than simply shipping raw beans overseas.

The effort comes as soybean farmers watch China closely. The country continues to shift toward cheaper Brazilian beans. 

“China has been a big buyer in the past. They will continue to be,” Schmitz said. “But we want to diversify.”  

Last year, fallout from President Donald Trump’s higher tariffs affected soybean farmers. China announced retaliatory tariffs, including an additional tariff on soybeans, and suspended imports. The dispute effectively dried up the Chinese market for South Dakota growers, after China previously purchased about 30% of the state’s soybeans.

Then, China agreed to suspend the retaliatory tariffs and to buy at least 12 million metric tons of U.S. soybeans in the last two months of 2025, and at least 25 million metric tons per year from 2026 through 2028. The Trump administration then rolled out a $12 billion farm aid package to help offset farmers’ losses from trade disruptions. 

Schmitz said China has so far bought what it agreed to buy, but that’s still about 10% less than previous levels. And because South Dakota is far from major ports and transportation costs are high, Schmitz said, federal trade assistance “has not made farmers here whole.”

Schmitz said the shortfall matters for farmers, who already face rising costs for fertilizer and fuel. That’s due in part to tariffs on imports and disruptions of markets for petroleum-based products caused by Trump’s war against Iran. 

Lt. Gov. Venhuizen said the delegation also received embassy briefings and met with foreign affairs officials. He said the mission was about building relationships, exchanging information and identifying openings for future deals. 

Venhuizen said one of the clearest opportunities is in food processing, which he said could help South Dakota create more value-added opportunities for South Dakota agricultural commodities. 

With the state’s help, CJ Schwan’s is constructing a $550 million food production plant in Sioux Falls. The company, which is part of the South Korean global conglomerate CJ Group, has also opened an office in downtown Sioux Falls.

Venhuizen said the delegation met with CJ Schwan’s in South Korea to discuss the status of the project. He said the company’s presence in South Dakota helped give the state credibility with other Korean companies. 

Following the trade mission, Fonkert announced his resignation from South Dakota Trade to “prioritize time with my family.” Fonkert, who held the job less than a year, succeeded Luke Lindberg, who now serves as U.S. Department of Agriculture undersecretary for trade and foreign affairs.

title: Iran war ‘not going well’ strategically,  former NATO commander tells SD audience
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Former NATO Supreme Allied Commander James Stavridis speaks to an audience at Augustana University in Sioux Falls Thursday. Photo by Joshua Haiar, South Dakota Searchlight
_full_text:
Iran war ‘not going well’ strategically,  former NATO commander tells SD audience
Former NATO Supreme Allied Commander James Stavridis speaks to an audience at Augustana University in Sioux Falls Thursday. Photo by Joshua Haiar, South Dakota Searchlight 

SIOUX FALLS — Former NATO Supreme Allied Commander James Stavridis told an audience Thursday evening at Augustana University that the war with Iran is “not going well” strategically, and America’s leaders are stretched too thin with conflicts around the globe. 

Stavridis also described Ukraine as a grinding war likely headed toward a disappointing settlement, China as a long-term strategic test, and Venezuela as a place where the recent U.S. removal of the country’s dictator was strategically risky but could produce an opening for democratic reforms.

With its involvement in those and other international matters at the same time, Stavridis said, the Trump administration has created a “bandwidth” problem for the few people President Donald Trump has entrusted to handle each situation.

“It’s too much, and they don’t have the professional support that they need,” Stavridis said. 

Stavridis was the longest-serving combatant commander in recent U.S. history. He is a retired four-star U.S. Navy admiral who oversaw operations in Afghanistan, Libya, Syria, the Balkans and counter-piracy efforts off the coast of Africa. He also led operations across Latin America.

Stavridis was the featured speaker for Augustana University’s Boe Forum on Public Affairs at the Elmen Center athletic arena. 

Iran

“We’re a month into it,” Stavridis said of the war against Iran. “How’s it going? It’s not going well, in my view.” 

Stavridis said the war has gone better militarily than strategically. He said U.S. strikes have been effective at hitting their targets, with 4 goals in mind: “snip the nuclear capability,” “beat down the Iranian Revolutionary Guard,” “take out the Iranian navy,” and “take out the ballistic missiles.”

“Our strikes are very effective, and we’re quite good at this,” he said, aside from fatal errors, including a Tomahawk missile that hit an Iranian elementary school adjacent to a military base. 

“But strategically, how’s it going?” he said. “Not very well.” 

Stavridis said the military successes have not resolved the larger problem: Iran still has ways to impose pain, such as controlling the Strait of Hormuz by attacking oil and gas shipments passing through it. That has driven oil prices higher, and “the Trump administration is trying to get out of this,” Stavridis said. “They’re trying to stand down. They want to find a solution.”

But Stavridis said Trump cannot end the war by declaring victory and walking away, because “you may not be interested in war, but war is interested in you,” meaning both sides must agree to stop launching attacks for a war to end. 

Stavridis said his best guess is that there’s a “70% chance” the war ends by the U.S. promising to stop the bombing, Iran releasing its grip on the Strait of Hormuz, and both sides leaving Iran’s efforts to build nuclear weapons to be addressed later. 

“That would be sufficient for Trump to declare a win,” Stavridis said. “He’ll say it’s the greatest victory in military history; whatever,” he added, to which the audience erupted in laughter and applause. He added that the conflict is costing the U.S. about $2 billion a day.

Stavridis said there is a 30% chance of a prolonged war. Stavridis said in that scenario, Iran would keep pressuring global energy markets and activate terror networks throughout the Middle East, Europe and the United States. 

He said putting boots on the ground in Iran would be a mistake that would increase the likelihood that the war escalates and continues. 

“For that, you need a big plan, a big strategy,” he said. “They have neither.” 

Ukraine

Stavridis said Ukraine has survived because of its own fighting spirit against Russia’s invasion, reinforced by Western weapons, sanctions on Russia and NATO’s backing. 

“It’s really the war of Putin’s ego,” he said of Russian dictator Vladimir Putin. 

“You can forget this idea that he purports of histories, destiny and Ukraine ‘has always been part of Mother Russia, and the Rodina,” Stavridis said, referencing a Russian term for the homeland. “That’s a crock.”

He said what Putin wants is the country’s natural resources. 

Stavridis is pessimistic about a clean Ukrainian victory. He said Russia controls 20% of Ukraine’s territory, having invaded from the east, and an end to the war will likely include Ukraine giving up that land, “and I hate it.” Though Stavridis added that Ukraine would likely join NATO shortly after. 

“And believe me, we want them in NATO,” he said, to which the audience again applauded. 

Stavridis said negotiations could come soon, given that Russia and Ukraine are both “burning through troops.”

Stavridis added that Putin “wants desperately” to build an alliance with China, “and he’s kind of getting there.” 

China

Stavridis said U.S. tensions with China span trade, cyber attacks, threats to Taiwan’s independence, and China’s militarization of the South China Sea, including its buildup of artificial islands that function like mini-bases.

Stavridis said Taiwan must become a “porcupine,” meaning well-armed and hard to conquer, to deter China from invading. He said Taiwan’s military lacks the modern weapons it needs. 

But Stavridis said he does not see a U.S. military conflict with China as inevitable. Instead, he suggested the goal should be competition without tipping into war.

“I think we will probably get there,” he said of that balance.

Venezuela

Stavridis said the U.S. operation that removed the former Venezuelan dictator Nicolás Maduro was militarily impressive, but that the harder and more important test is what comes next. 

“You can debate the legality of it, or the strategy of it, but as a military operation, quite extraordinary,” he said.

Stavridis said removing Maduro has resulted in a, “shall we say, cooperative regime.” 

He suggested that the best-case scenario is a transition that leads toward free and fair elections. 

“And then, you have the opportunity to really flip the table in Latin America,” he said. “It’s not a terrible outcome.”

title: Commissioners caution against giving timeline for property tax relief
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: Gov. Larry Rhoden discusses property tax relief with state legislators. Rhoden introduced property tax relief legislation that passed this year. But Meade County Commissioner Talbot Wieczorek cautioned about giving any timelines for relief, since the legislation involves instituting a county-wide sales tax. SouthDakota Searchlight photo
_full_text:
Commissioners caution against giving timeline for property tax relief
Gov. Larry Rhoden discusses property tax relief with state legislators. Rhoden introduced property tax relief legislation that passed this year. But Meade County Commissioner Talbot Wieczorek cautioned about giving any timelines for relief, since the legislation involves instituting a county-wide sales tax. SouthDakota Searchlight photo 

STURGIS — County officials need to be careful about not giving property owners a timeline for when they will see property tax relief related to a county sales tax. 

On Tuesday, Meade County Commissioner Talbot Wieczorek cautioned Treasurer Robin Shrake about giving any timelines for when residents will start to see property tax reductions related to Senate Bill 96, which permits the county to charge a half percent sales tax. The new sales tax is intended to reduce counties’ reliance on property tax for their general fund budget, allowing for cuts at the local level. The legislation requires the extra money to be deposited into a property tax reduction fund. 

Gov. Larry Rhoden proposed the bill and signed it into law on March 12. Rhoden’s office estimates that it will result in 10-25% in property tax reductions, or about $660 per homeowner,  in counties that implement the sales tax. 

Counties are not permitted to charge sales tax until Senate Bill 96 takes effect, July 1. Before the half percent tax can be implemented, Wieczorek said the county needs to pass an enabling ordinance, which it cannot do until the legislation goes into effect. After that, he said there is a 6-month delay with the South Dakota Department of Revenue. The earliest the county would be able to collect a new, half-cent sales tax to offset property taxes, is Jan. 1, and that will only happen if the enabling ordinance is not referred to a vote. 

“The moral of the story is to be very careful telling anybody when this is going to occur,” Wieczorek said. 

The state sales tax is currently 4.2%, but temporary sales tax relief is scheduled to expire this year, which means it will go up to 4.5%. During the 2026 Legislative session, lawmakers authorized the additional .3% to go into a dedicated fund for property tax relief. On top of the state sales tax, municipalities can charge a 2% sales tax, with an additional 1% tax on alcohol sales, attraction and event ticket sales, lodging, and restaurant purchases. Another proposal, House Bill 1245, allows municipalities to charge a 1% temporary sales tax for special projects. 

Overall, starting next year, South Dakotans who live in areas that take full advantage of the new county and city tax laws, could pay as much as 9% in sales tax, with .8% of that specifically dedicated to property tax relief.

title: 4 vehicle accident near Deadwood results in casualty, critical injuries
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: DEADWOOD — First responders were called to an accident at mile marker 44 on US Highway 14A Friday night involving 4 vehicles that resulted in one casualty, critical injuries, and other victims being transported to hospitals.
_full_text:
DEADWOOD — First responders were called to an accident at mile marker 44 on US Highway 14A Friday night involving 4 vehicles that resulted in one casualty, critical injuries, and other victims being transported to hospitals. 

Joel Ellis, chief of the Deadwood Volunteer Fire Department, said that he was the first on the scene in his personal vehicle after receiving a page at 7:55 p.m. 

“There was one deceased on arrival, we had 2 that were critical,” he said. “We did bring in 2 separate helicopters, one out of Hot Springs and one out of Rapid that transported 2 individuals that were critical.”

Ellis said other non-critically injured victims were transported to Monument Health in Spearfish, and one individual that walked away with a twisted ankle. 

Ellis said Deadwood Volunteer Fire Department responded with Engine 1, Rescue 3, Truck 4, and Brush 6. 

“We assisted with the triage of patients, we had firefighters driving ambulances, and we had firefighters assisting medics inside the ambulance,” Ellis said. 

Ellis said firefighters assisted by blocking Highway 14A further east in Boulder Canyon near the Cedar Rose Spa and another at the intersection of Highway 14A and Highway 85.

Ellis said responding agencies included Deadwood Police Department, Lawrence County Sheriff's Department, Deadwood Monument Health Ambulance, Spearfish Ambulance, Lawrence County Emergency Management, Black Hills Life Flight, and the Lawrence County Coroner. 

“It was a mass casualty incident, but all of the agencies that were there worked very, very well together,” said Ellis. “From an incident standpoint, it went as well as it could. I think our community should be proud of the responding agencies that were out there, because it was a difficult scene.”

The Pioneer will continue to report on the incident as more information becomes available.

title: Whitewood Elementary principal resigns
byline: By Brandon Woltkamp Black Hills Pioneer
description: Brit Porterfield, principal at Whitewood Elementary school, resigned Thursday. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Whitewood Elementary principal resigns
Brit Porterfield, principal at Whitewood Elementary school, resigned Thursday. Pioneer file photo 

WHITEWOOD — The Whitewood Elementary School Principal Brit Porterfield, resigned Thursday effective immediately.

Porterfield had been with the Meade School District since 2019 as a part-time STEM teacher and part-time principal of Whitewood Elementary. 

Porterfield was instrumental in the Homeschool Connections program — bringing in roughly $100,000 — for the Meade School District. 

In November 2025 with a proposal to combine the Whitewood and rural schools principal positions, Whitewood residents pressed the school board to keep the positions separate. With nearly a $1 million budget deficit, the board ultimately decided to combine the positions.

Porterfield was a popular teacher among the community and was presented with accolades and awards including the Dan Swets Robotics Materials Award in 2024 and led Whitewood Elementary in 2022 to be a distinguished school under the National Elementary and Secondary Education Act (ESEA). 

Porterfield declined immediate comment but is planning on making a comment in the near future.

Shelly Mikkelson, a former teacher with the Belle Fourche School District for 20 years and principal of 2 years for the Meade School District rural schools, replace Porterfield effective Friday. 

Meade School District Wayne Wormstadt declined comment.

title: Black Hills PIoneer at 150: A Rare Tablet
description: The Black Hills Pioneer is the oldest continuously operating business in West River Dakota Territory; and 2026 marks our 150th year of telling the stories of the Black Hills. Over time our newspaper has absorbed the titles of numerous other publications, and we would like to share some of the more compelling stories from those early editions.
_full_text:
BHPioneer150

The Black Hills Pioneer
The Black Hills Pioneer is the oldest continuously operating business in West River Dakota Territory; and 2026 marks our 150th year of telling the stories of the Black Hills. Over time our newspaper has absorbed the titles of numerous other publications, and we would like to share some of the more compelling stories from those early editions.
 Every Saturday we will be publishing stories from our archives, focusing heavily on the gold rush era of the 1800s, starting with our very first publication on June 8, 1876. These stories are re-printed exactly as they were first published, leaving in place the historic typos, grammar, style and terminology – politically correct or otherwise. It is our belief that history is sacrosanct and should never be edited to reflect the societal norms of the present.
 We hope you enjoy this historic journey. It is an honor and a privilege to be a trusted permanent record keeper of the Black Hills community; we look forward to the next 150 years of history in the making in the magnificent Black Hills.

~ Black Hills Weekly Times • March 19, 1887 ~

A Rare Tablet,

Concerning the First Visitation to the Black Hills, by Intrepid Whites.

No portion of the great western continent is so rich in legend, incident and event, as that known as the Black Hills of Dakota. Until a very recent date the very heart of the Indian country, sacredly guarded by a nation of the most populous, barbarous and blood-thirsty savages, it enjoyed still greater immunity from invasion by reason of the expansive, uninviting waste designated on charts and improperly estimated by all as “the great American desert,” far within which this realm of hidden wealth reposed. That an oasis existed in the Sahara of the west was indefinitely known to frontiersmen long before that mythical line designated “the border,” had reached the Missouri, but such knowledge was vague and altogether unsatisfactory. Now and then incoming Sioux, ladened with furs and precious metals were questioned as to the source of their booty, but with significant shrug of shoulders, and indefinite glance toward the setting sun, they pressed their lips still closer and moved along. Occasionally a more condescending, if not more intelligent half breed or squaw man, would hint of a beautiful land; of majestic mountains; of streams of clearest, purest health restoring water; of defiles, and parks and lakes; of a wealth of luxuriant foliage and bright tinted, odorous flowers, all of which commingled and presented a scene of the most picturesque character. The streams were curbed with gold; the towering mountains contained all manner of metals; game of all kinds roamed throughout the marvelous region harmless to each other and unharmed by the Sioux who held the locality sacred as the abiding place of the Great Spirit. Such were the Hills outlined but never were located. They were merely—“way off there.” Then came an occasional statement from the Jesuits, also unsatisfactory at first, but reaching tangibility by degrees until Father DeSmet supplied the first accurate information, in 1828. In an unguarded moment he let drop the secret of the isolated realm of wealth, and thence on the whites maintained determined effort to penetrate the mysterious locally. If jealous of the Hills before, the Indians were doubly so after their secret was betrayed, and for over fifty years fought with all the craftiness and determination of the race to hold the domain inviolate.

The first exploration of the Hills by white men is unknown and never will be determined. The great California stampede of ’49 passed a couple of hundred miles south, and it is said that a number of small parties therefrom visited this locality. If so such expeditions resulted disastrously, for nothing was ever heard of them. A few years later government exploring parties passed along the foothills, but it was not until 1874 that anything definite was known of the interior. From that date to properly begins a record of the occupation of this favored portion of Dakota. Now and then discoveries are made indicative of explorations and prospecting by whites years and years ago, but such are silent, uncommunicative souvenirs attesting the one undisputable fact, but giving no details. A rusty, stockless pistol; the deeply blazed tree; the deserted cabin and other discoveries appear as monuments to the intrepid explorer, but to whom and of what period, there is no record.

On Monday the number of relics of by-gone days are early adventures was increased to a very interesting extent by the discovery of a remarkable tablet. While roaming over the prairie west of Lookout mountain, near Spearfish, Mr. Louis Stone (Thoen) unearthed a sand stone about ten inches square, upon which in irregular characters had been cut, evidently with a knife this inscription:

CAME TO THE HILLS IN 1833 7 OF US.

EMILE AUGUST

EZRA KIND

E. W. HOOD

P. BROWN

R. KENT

WILLIAM KING

INDIAN CROW

ALL DED BUT ME EZRA KIND.

KILLED BY INDIANS BEYOND THE HIGH HILL. THEY GOT ALL OF OUR GOLD. JUNE 1834.

The tablet was found beneath a heavy stone imbedded some distance in the ground, and bore evidence of having rested in the one position for years. The inscription was, evidently cut with a knife, a long time ago, and was in places partly effaced by action of the elements. Nothing else of significance was found. The tablet is a valuable addition to the archaeology of the Black Hills, and may lead to important developments.

Editor’s note:

(From BHP Jan. 14, 2000)

The Thoen Stone was named for Louis Thoen who discovered the stone at the foot of Lookout Mountain, near Spearfish on March 14, 1887. Although some scholars dispute the authenticity of the stone, the late historian Frank Thomson devoted much of life researching the history of the men named on the stone. His research added more credibly to the likelihood that the stone is genuine.

According to Frank Thomson, a group of men left Independence, Missouri with the intention of opening a route to Santa Fe, New Mexico during the spring of 1883. while on the trail, they met an American Indian from the Crow tribe who knew of the existence of gold in the Black Hills. It was the Crow guide who led the expedition into the Black Hills.

The only record that exists as to the fate of the explorers is the Thoen Stone. 

Although no one knows what happened Kind, it is assumed he died in the Black Hills. For if he had ever made it back to civilization, history might have been rewritten with the Black Hills gold rush preceding the California rush.

In the years following the discovery of the Thoen Stone, Frank Thomson located the descendants of several members of the ill-fated party. All of the descendants confirmed that their ancestors headed west in 1833 and were never heard of again.

The members of the ill-fated Ezra Kind party may have been the first gold seekers to enter the mysterious Black Hills, but they would not be the last.

Since 1876, the Black Hills Pioneer has been a locally owned & operated newspaper.

title: Lead historic preservation recognizes 5 homes
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: LEAD — Restoration work for historic homes are ongoing in Lead, and recently the Lead Historic Preservation Commission recognized five homeowners, whose efforts have contributed to the timeless beauty of their historic homes.
_full_text:
LEAD — Restoration work for historic homes are ongoing in Lead, and recently the Lead Historic Preservation Commission recognized five homeowners, whose efforts have contributed to the timeless beauty of their historic homes.   

The historic homes awards come with a bronze plaque that the homeowners can place on the outside of the property, as well as a historical accounting of the building’s roots in the community. 

In February, the commission recognized Jacque Fuller, owner of the Glover House at 11 Glenville Dr. Built in 1899, the house was the subject of much speculation that it would be a dwelling for the Christian Science Church, because the man who built it was a Christian Scientist. But when construction was complete, owner Mary Baker Eddy announced that the $15,000 residence would be a gift to her son, George W. Glover, who came to the Black Hills in 1878. Glover began a full time career as a prospector and miner in 1889. He located the Tilton and Victor mines in the Bald Mountain District, which he sold to the Golden Reward Company for nearly $40,000. In 1900, he became the general manager of the Golden Reward Company, which owned several valuable mining properties in the Black Hills. 

The John Moodie House, located at 17 Third St., was also recognized on March 18. Built in 1938, the house has been owned by the Regan family since 1964. Dan and Trudy Regan said they purchased the home from Dan’s father in 1975, sold it in 1980, and then bought it back in 1983. The house is characterized by its flat roof, which is part of its international style — a modern, architectural movement known for its functionalism and minimalism, which was popular in the 1920s. 

“Whoever built flat roofs in Lead should have had their head examined,” said Dan Regan. 

He added that when the house was originally built, the roof leaked badly. So the owners added a 6-inch parapet, and sloped the roof to drain through the center. The problem was later fixed with a composite roof, Regan said. 

Regan said his father purchased the house form John Moodie, who was the home’s original owner. A 1923 graduate of Lead High School, Moodie was the business manager for the Ford Tri-Motor Barnstorming Tour, piloted by Clyde Ice, in 1928. He also headed up the state gasoline tax department in Pierre, and eventually moved back to Lead in 1932, where he worked as personnel manager for Homestake until 1967. According to a historical account of the house that Smith compiled, during World War II Moodie flew with the Civil Air Patrol to provide courier services in South Dakota. He was also the executive director of the S.D. State Aeronautics Association in 1945. 

The Cotton House, located at 105 Paul St., was nearly razed in 2018 because it was in such disrepair. At that time, the house was sold at public auction for $30,000, with proceeds of the auction donated to the Lead Historic Preservation Commission. Since then, owners Tom Johnson and Gail Parfrey, have been working with David Gackle, of Odd Jobs Construction, to completely restore the dwelling. 

Built in 1901, the Cotton House was considered one of the most modern and fancy houses in Lead. The home offered electricity, centralized hot water heat, and plumbing, and the décor in the woodwork, staircase, and windows was considered among the most elegant in town. It belonged to James Cotton, who made his fortune selling liquor at Cotton’s Place. 

The Gilroy house was the next house to be recognized. Located at 113 Durango St., it was built in 1902 and expanded to include three lots over the years. The current owner is Mike Hemiller. Original ownership of the Victorian-style home is unknown, but at one point Lead Mayor John Gilroy purchased the property for $1,200. Before Gilroy became the mayor of Lead, he was an executive in the Hearst Mercantile stores, and in 1901 he purchased a grocery business from Ernest May. 

The Mike Cain house, built in 1899, was the final historic structure to be recognized. The Queen Ann-style house that Ashlyn Pearson owns, is located at 840 West Main St. An Irishman who made his way to America from Australia, Cain studied mining in California. In 1886 he came to the Black Hills, and worked in the blacksmith department of Homestake. Cain was a major real estate developer in Lead, who built a block of businesses known as the “Cain Block” worth $15,000, that was once located at the intersection of Mill and Main Streets. The businesses were demolished due to subsidence in the 1930s. 

Terry Smith, president of the Lead Historic Preservation Commission, said since 2020 the commission has given out 15 of these awards. Eventually, Smith said, the historic preservation commission plans to create an online urban trail map, which includes a walking tour of Lead’s historic homes.

title: Sturgis city councilman resigns
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: Nick Jones
_full_text:
Sturgis city councilman resigns
Nick Jones 

STURGIS — Sturgis city Councilman Nick Jones has resigned. 

Jones was elected to represent Ward 1 in April 2024. His term was scheduled to expire in 2027. 

Mayor pro tempore Tony Dargatz said Jones indicated to the city that his family will move to Texas in June. His resignation is effective May 19, which is the day after the last council meeting in May. 

“Serving on the council and representing Ward 1 — the ward where I grew up — has been a meaningful honor, and I will always cherish the opportunity,” Jones wrote in his letter. “Thank you to everyone who placed their trust in me. It has been an honor to serve alongside my fellow council members and on behalf of the residents of Ward 1.” 

The Sturgis City Council scheduled a special meeting for Friday evening to discuss a special election to fill Jones’ seat on the council. According to South Dakota Codified Law 9-13-14.1 the council may fill a seat on the council with a special election. A staff report attached to Friday’s special meeting agenda indicates that the Ward 1 council seat could be included on the ballot for the special mayoral election that has already been scheduled for June 2. However, the staff report states that in order to meet the public notice requirements, the council must act immediately to make a decision about the special election. 

Jones’ resignation comes on the heels of Mayor Kevin Forrester’s decision to step down from his mayor’s seat, due to health reasons related to pancreatic cancer. The Sturgis City Council met in special session on Wednesday to discuss next steps for a special mayoral election to fill Forrester’s seat. Jones was present at the meeting but did not mention his resignation.  

Jones’ resignation letter is dated for Monday, March 23. The city did not announce the resignation until Thursday afternoon. Dargatz said that’s because they did not find out until Thursday. 

Jones did not respond to messages seeking comment about his resignation before press time.

title: Overdose deaths decline in SD
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: The number of South Dakotans who died from drug overdoses continued to decrease in 2025, but many of those deaths were potentially preventable, according to data presented Wednesday to the state Opioid Advisory Committee.
_full_text:
The number of South Dakotans who died from drug overdoses continued to decrease in 2025, but many of those deaths were potentially preventable, according to data presented Wednesday to the state Opioid Advisory Committee.

Drug overdose deaths decreased to 83 in 2025, according to provisional data, down from 94 in 2024 and significantly down from a peak of 104 in 2021. Fentanyl-related overdose deaths decreased by two last year, and meth overdose deaths were cut in half — down from 42 to 19.

Opioid-related deaths were the only category to increase, growing from 39 in 2024 to 41 in 2025.

Of the 2025 overdose deaths, 68 are labeled by the state as unintentional or intent unknown. Sixty-two percent of those victims were white, and the same percentage were male victims. Thirty-seven percent of those overdose death cases were caused by fentanyl, followed by meth at 28%. 

Fifty-eight percent of victims of unintentional or intent unknown overdose deaths were between the ages of 25 and 44. That is the “young, working population” mostly affected, said Amanda Weinzetl, with the state Department of Health.

Of those who died from overdose, 25% worked as a construction or trade worker. Another 19% were unemployed and 13% worked in food service.

Opioids made up a larger share of overdose deaths in the northeast and southeast regions of South Dakota between 2020 and 2024, according to the latest data from the State Unintentional Drug Overdose Reporting System. Stimulants, especially meth, were a larger problem in western and central South Dakota.

Over 60% of overdose deaths in that timeframe had at least one opportunity for intervention, Weinzetl said, while 43% of the unintentional overdose deaths happened with a potential bystander nearby, and 21% involved a victim with a known mental health diagnosis.

Those are among the reasons the state is pushing to distribute thousands of kits containing naloxone, an opioid-overdose antidote, to counties across the state. The effort began last year as a partnership with the nonprofit Emily’s Hope and had distributed 22,416 naloxone kits as of February. Distribution sites range from emergency rooms to repurposed magazine racks in hospital lobbies, libraries, grocery stores, gas stations and other high-traffic, temperature-controlled community hubs.

Vanessa Barnes, with the state Department of Social Services, said prevention efforts have to be widespread since overdose deaths are largely unpredictable.

Some areas of the state are oversaturated: the Sioux Falls area has 6,215 more naloxone medications freely available than research suggests it needs, and Pennington County, which includes Rapid City, has 1,542 more than it needs. The state needs to distribute 3,624 more naloxone medications across 48 counties to reach its target.

title: 3 SD governor  debates scheduled
byline: By Carson Walker South Dakota News Watch
description: South Dakotans will have three chances in the next month to watch the four Republican candidates for governor take part in debates that will be televised statewide.
_full_text:
SDNewsWatchLogo

South Dakotans will have three chances in the next month to watch the four Republican candidates for governor take part in debates that will be televised statewide.

Aberdeen businessman Toby Doeden, state House Speaker Jon Hansen, U.S. Rep Dusty Johnson and Gov. Larry Rhoden are vying for the GOP nomination in the June 2 primary.

“Voters need to make an educated decision when they go to the ballot box, and the best way to do that is to question our candidates and have them respond,” said Cara Hetland, director of journalism content at SDPB, which has produced televised forums since the 1970s.

“You can’t get that just from someone’s social media feed. And they need to be held accountable for their record.”

Times, locations and moderators

Here are details of each event:

4 p.m. CT Tuesday, March 31: KELO-TV will air its debate live from its Sioux Falls studios, with anchors Dan Santella and Lauren Soulek moderating. It will also be re-aired and available on demand on the station’s broadcast and streaming platforms.  

8 p.m. CT Monday, April 13: South Dakota Public Broadcasting’s forum from its Sioux Falls studios will be moderated by SDPB’s Jackie Hendry and South Dakota News Watch’s Alexander Rifaat. A limited number of tickets will be available for an in-person audience. SDPB will archive the forum on its YouTube channel immediately afterward.

7:30 p.m. CT Monday, April 27: The Dakota Scout and Forum Communications will host the final debate in front of an audience of subscribers at the Belbas Theater in the Washington Pavilion in Sioux Falls. It will be moderated by Dakota Scout co-founder Jonathan Ellis and Patrick Lalley, editor of Sioux Falls Live, and broadcast on Forum’s TV stations, KSFL-TV in Sioux Falls and KNBN-TV in Rapid City. Forum also owns the Mitchell Republic, Pierre Capital Journal and Madison Daily Leader newspapers. The debate will be available online afterward.  

SDPB will also air forums for other primary contests if more than one candidate qualifies for the ballot.

Veteran and new media players

Anna Peters, Keloland Media Group’s director of news and multimedia content, said the station received a flood of viewer questions that will be asked of the candidates.

“It’s one of the most important things we can do as a TV station, a news station. This gives the voters a chance to see the candidates, hear what they have to say and see how they interact because, honestly, sometimes that interaction during a debate can help decide a race,” she said.

Peters said she looks forward to seeing how many people tune in live on their smartphones because that audience is growing.

While KELO-TV and SDPB have televised candidate debates for decades, this is a first for The Dakota Scout and Forum Communications in South Dakota.

Lalley and Ellis worked together at the Argus Leader newspaper in Sioux Falls with Joe Sneve, the Scout’s other co-founder, who organized the event. The idea came from his time as a city beat reporter at the Argus when he live-streamed city council forums. He wanted to bring that format to the Scout, which launched in 2022.

“The fact we got all four candidates to a debate shows how the Scout has grown in its influence,” Sneve said.

“I think it demonstrates the credibility of the players here,” Lalley added. “Our reputations were built in the mainstream media at the Argus Leader. And one of the reasons we’ve been able to do what we do is because of that reputation.” 

Sneve said the debate builds on the Scout and Forum’s working relationship.  

“I don’t know that we would have gotten the invitations to do something like this if we weren’t also offering a broadcast arm, which is where Forum and Sioux Falls Live come in,” he said. “They print our paper. We run their sports content in our printed product. They’re building out a broadcast division in South Dakota, so it felt like a perfect fit.”

South Dakota News Watch is an independent nonprofit. Read, donate and subscribe for free at sdnewswatch.org.
