title: Affordable peace of mind: Lawrence County adopts Land Notification Alert system:  a weapon to fight deed fraud
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Affordable peace of mind Lawrence County adopts Land Notification Alert system:  a weapon to fight deed fraud
Courtesy photo 

DEADWOOD — Lawrence County has taken a step to protect landowners from seller impersonation fraud, the latest fraud threatening property owners across the country. 

Davida Hansen, Lawrence County Register of Deeds, told county commissioners Tuesday that her office has received 4 or 5 calls in the last 12 months from people reporting “for sale” signs being placed on their property without their consent. The incidents are part of the latest trend in real estate fraud, where fraudsters impersonate sellers to try and illegally sell property, oftentimes disappearing with the buyer’s money before law enforcement can track their true identity. In response, Hansen’s office has signed a 5-year contract with TriMin, leveraging their Land Notification Alerts software. The platform will alert registered users if any document related to their property has been filed with Hansen’s office. Lawrence County will become the tenth county in the state to utilize the program.

‘Reactive, not proactive’

Gregory Dias, director of information systems and technology for the county, said that the system doesn’t prevent fraud — it simply alerts landowners and shortens the time between the incident and beginning an investigation. 

“It’s more reactive than proactive,” said Dias. “Let’s say, for example, that I’m the owner of a property and someone tries to maliciously and fraudulently transfer the ownership of my property to somebody else. It will show me as the guarantor because I’m the property owner, so I’ll get a notification. I can say, ‘Wait a minute, I’m not doing any transfer of a property to anyone.’ Then I could contact the Register of Deeds office and do some inquiring as to what the document is and who it came from.”

Dias said the system will automatically upload all reregistered documents from the county’s secure database over a determined amount of time over a secure connection. He said the platform will not be accessible to fraudsters wishing to stop notifications or upload fake documents. 

Dias said landowners wanting to register for the service will need to go directly to the Lawrence County Register of Deed’s webpage, found at https://www.lawrence.sd.us/171/Register-of-Deeds, after the software is installed and running, to register for the Land Notification Alert service. 

“There could be a rogue website out there, there’s always a chance of that,” said Dias. “So, it’s important to go to the county website and avoid any sponsored links from a Google search. In this case, unless you have a direct link already saved, it’s always a good idea to go to a known safe website and use the link there.”

Once the alert system is placed on the county website, people interested in using the system will have to choose Lawrence County from the list of counties using the service in South Dakota. After clicking the link, users will be required to register with a username and password. Users will be able to choose whether they are alerted via text message or email of any filed documents relating to their property.

The service will come at no cost to landowners.

For the first year, the county is paying the $3,800 annual fee with South Dakota Modernization and Preservation funds. 

Hansen and Dias said that they hope that the software will be up and running within a month. 

Seller impersonation fraud

Lauren Beyersdorf, director of client services at Black Hills Title Company in Spearfish, said that realtors and title companies are on the front lines of preventing seller impersonation fraud. Beyersdorf said that she’s attended or spoke at nearly 20 seminars, conventions, or meetings in the last 2 to 3 years, strengthening her education so that she can pass that knowledge to real estate agencies and other title companies in the area. 

“When we’re talking about seller impersonation fraud, that’s when someone impersonates the seller to sell the property under their name,” said Beyersdorf. “They’re not looking to keep the property; they’re looking for the proceeds from that sale. And by the time that those documents are already of record, that transfer has already been made to (the fraudster).”

Beyersdorf said bad actors are typically looking for property that is either vacant land or non-owner-occupied secondary homes or vacation rentals with no associated debts.

“They’re looking for homes where there’s not someone actually living there,” she said. “If a real estate agent shows up at my door and says, ‘Oh, hey, you’re selling your property. I’m going to put my sign in your yard.’ And I’m like, ‘I didn’t contact you.’ They don’t want that.”

Beyersdorf said property owners who live outside of South Dakota have received phone calls from their neighbors, reporting for sale signs being placed on their property.

“They’ll say, ‘Hey, I noticed you’re selling, I didn’t know you were looking to sell,’” she said. “And owners will say, ‘I’m not. I’m not selling.’ That’s when we get calls or the Register of Deeds gets calls.”

Beyersdorf said fraudsters will look for properties that have a different address than the owner’s address or tax mailing address, with no outstanding mortgages or liens. Once they find the property, they contact realtors and ask to put the property up for sale.

Beyersdorf said these “sellers” will typically refuse a video call, will push for a quick, cash sale, and could be willing to accept offers for less than the property is worth. 

“They don’t want to have to wait for anyone, they want the sure deal,” she said. “So, there’s usually a lot of pressure and social engineering. I’ve heard an agent say that an individual called them who wants to sell and their spouse or their mother has cancer, and they need to sell it right away.”

Beyersdorf said fraudulent sellers could refuse to attend signings, claiming to be out of the country, could demand that the proceeds be wired, and will want to use their own notary. 

“Those are some of the red flags that we continue to have agents pay attention to. If this seller is checking all the boxes, we want them to pause,” she said. “Verify the sellers identification. Have them send the driver’s license, send it to us, do all of the checks and balances and make sure that they’re talking to the right person.”

Beyersdorf said fraudsters are easily able to look up notaries in the area, purchase a matching stamp online, and forge the notaries’ signature. Fake drivers’ licenses are easily purchased online to match the legitimate property owner’s name. She said that because Black Hills Title Company used to be one of the only title companies in the area, they have archives of buyers and sellers’ drivers’ licenses, phone numbers, and signatures that they can compare to documents fraudulent sellers are providing to real estate agencies. 

In the last 2 years, Beyersdorf said that Black Hills Title Company, as well as real estate agencies and other title companies in the area, have had to change the way they validate buyers and sellers’ information. 

“We live in this zero-trust era where our office has to really vet every person’s identity,” she said. “We use a fraud protection platform so that if a seller isn’t going to be attending closing and they want to use their own services, we have them go through an entire verification of identity process. They take pictures, we track their IP address, it shows how long their email (and phone number) has been in existence. In general, title companies have really had to switch gears.”

Beyersdorf said that while real estate agents and title companies are flagging potential sellers regularly, she doesn’t know of any attempts of fraud that have been successful in Lawrence County.

Pennington County case

There have been reports of one case of seller impersonation fraud in Pennington County. 

Audra Casteel, register of deeds for Pennington County, said the fraudulent document was discovered during the recording process before it was recorded and affected the property’s chain of title.

“Deed fraud is an issue across the country,” said Casteel. “It is important for individuals to stay vigilant on their property, much like one would their bank account. Land Notification and other property alert software give an individual a way to keep an eye on their properties without having to be in the recording office on a daily basis. If an owner notices their property on a listing page, it is recommend that you contact your Register of Deeds office immediately of suspected fraudulent activity.”

As of print time, it is unclear what happened to the buyer of the property, the seller, and the original owner of the land. Casteel declined requests for further comment.

Locking your land

Beyersdorf and Hansen said that they both get regular calls from people who have seen ads on TV or have received pamphlets in the mail, telling them to “lock their land” to avoid deed fraud. 

“Land records are open to the public,” said Hansen. “It’s state law.”

Hansen said that anyone can visit the register of deeds office in Deadwood and research, request, and print documents. People are charged $1 per page to print documents. 

“In Lawrence County, you have to come into the office and research the property. We do not have anything online,” Hansen said. “So, they’re open as far as it is within the office, you have to come in and research the property and get the document number. But they’re open to the public.”

Beyersdorf said that it’s impossible to “lock your land.”

“You can’t go up to the Register of Deeds and say, ‘Don’t accept any more recordings on my property,’” said Beyersdorf. “They are public officials. We take documents up to them and they put them of record for the public, for you and I. You can go up there and you could research everything that’s on my property, you could go in and look at the books.”

Beyersdorf said that while property owners can’t stop a Register of Deeds office from recording documents, most people have an owner’s policy of title insurance that is paid for with a one-time payment at the time of closing. 

“We insure our health with medical, life, dental, all of that. This is title insurance, an insurance policy protecting your property rights,” Beyersdorf said. “Your right to enjoy the property, the right to access the property. Obviously, there can be some constraints in terms of covenants and easements and documents that are of record that could restrict owners. That owner’s policy protects you against cases of fraud, deed fraud, errors in the public record, that could cause an issue.”

Beyersdorf said the policy can protect the owner’s and their heirs to the property against any claims that could occur after the property is sold pertaining to the period of time when they owned the property in the past, as well. 

“The owner’s policy is a very small percentage of the premium to purchase property,” said Beyersorf. “It’s based off the purchase price of the property, and it is a very small fee in terms of purchasing a home in general.”

Beyersdorf said the claim is considered inexpensive because title companies are expected to do curative work up front, resulting in very few claims.

“We’ve shown you everything that’s on the property, everything that affects the property, and so our claims are low,” she said. “It’s not something that I get to deal with that often, if at all.”

Beyersdorf said most property owners should have that policy already in place, but it is an optional purchase. 

“We always say why not protect what is possibly the biggest investment of your life,” she said. “It’s a one-time fee, it’s not something that you pay every month like traditional insurances, and it’s typically paid at closing.”

Peace of mind

Beyersdorf and Hansen said that while the Land Notification Alert system is not preventative, it is an affordable “peace of mind” tool for the county and property owners. 

“I think that in terms of a timeframe, from time of action if something did happen where a transaction did go through and it was fraudulent, the real, vested property owner could then take action,” said Beyersdorf. “Call the title company, call the attorney that prepared the deed, start the process of working and getting that taken care of. I think real estate agents and title companies, we’re on the front lines of it and I think (the software) can be a good backup. But, again, if you have that policy protecting your property rights, we’re already there to help. We have the duty to defend and the obligation under that policy so that we could provide coverage.”

Hansen said that she’s hopeful that having the software in place will serve as a deterrent to fraudsters scouting the area. 

“I want to get something in place to safeguard in whatever way we can,” Hansen said. “I’m very excited to be ablet o offer this to the public and I’m very excited for (Dias) to get it set up so we can start using it. Hopefully it puts people’s minds at ease and gives them a little more assurance.”

title: Spearfish weighs merits of  city administrator position after  paying above-average wage for the role
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — Whether the city of Spearfish will employ a city administrator moving forward is an open question after parting ways with Bobby Falcon earlier this year.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — Whether the city of Spearfish will employ a city administrator moving forward is an open question after parting ways with Bobby Falcon earlier this year.

City Attorney David Knox said as much during a city council study session March 11.

The city of Spearfish had one of the highest-paid city administrators in the state before the position became vacant in February. However, the city paid its city administrator a competitive wage when compared with communities of similar size across the country. 

Falcon earned an annual salary of $197,000 after a raise was approved by the city council in October.

Spearfish, Pierre, Box Elder, Mitchell, Belle Fourche, and Lead all have or recently had city administrators. 

The average salary for city administrators in those communities is $146,632.16. Lead has the lowest-paid city administrator at $90,638.08. The highest paid currently employed city administrator in the South Dakota cities surveyed by the Pioneer is in Pierre, earning $162,069.66. That means, at the time of his departure, Falcon was the highest paid city administrator in any of the communities surveyed by the Pioneer by about $15,000. 

 SD chief administrative officer salaries 4x4.25.pdf
Note: Spearfish’s city administrator position is currently vacant. Data based on confirmed wage as of October 2025. City manager salaries are pictured in yellow. Administrator salaries are in blue. Black Hills Pioneer graphic

The salary range for any new city administrator in Spearfish would be $174,553- $265,592, set by the city’s adopted pay scale. The starting salary for the position in Spearfish is more than the current salary of the city administrators in Box Elder, Mitchell, Belle Fourche, and Lead.

Brookings, Vermillion, and Yankton have city managers, not city administrators. 

City managers have roles that are defined by state statute, and often retain responsibilities that other communities allocate to mayors and councils. The average city manager in Brookings, Vermillion, and Yankton has 13 years of experience in their position and earns an average of $192,453 annually. 

Brookings’ city manager earns the highest salary, at $224,102.99. Yankton pays the least at $175,791 — higher than the minimum salary would be for a new city administrator in Spearfish. Watertown and Aberdeen also have city managers, but the Pioneer did not hear back from Watertown by press time. Aberdeen required that the Pioneer pay to assemble salary data via a formal public records request. 

Huron, Sioux Falls, Rapid City, and Deadwood have neither a city manager nor a city administrator. Rapid City has a chief of staff, earning $127,307.96.

Looking only at South Dakota, Spearfish’s city administrator had a higher wage than most state government leaders. The governor, Larry Rhoden, earns $146,472.30, Lt. Gov. Tony Venhuizen earns $127,189.24, and Attorney General Marty Jackley earns $140,838.75.

 Regional leadership salaries 4x4.5.pdf
 Black Hills Pioneer graphic

Compared to regional executives, like the Spearfish superintendent of schools ($163,783.17) and the president of Black Hills State University ($310,000), the city administrator’s wage landed in the middle.

National comparison

If Spearfish is not only competing with other South Dakota communities for qualified city leaders, but rather hoping to draw in candidates from the rest of the country, then its wage range is on target. 

The Pioneer reached out to one community in each state, not including the District of Columbia and territories like Puerto Rico, with similar population sizes to Spearfish to determine if, on a national scale, its wage offering was comparable. Spearfish’s population is roughly 13,803 according to US Census data.

Out of 50 cities querried, 18 cities, including Spearfish, responded by press time. Three did not have city managers or administrators. In the remaining 15 cities with an average population size of 13,829, the average annual salary for a city administrator or a city manager was $179,015. 

Snoqualmie, Wash., for example, has a population of roughly 13,829. Its city administrator earns $241,978.20. Hardeeville, S.C., has a population of about 13,862. Its city manager earns $188,837. In Glenn Carbon Ill., the village administrator earns $162,889.00. That community’s population is 13,883.

How the pay scale was set

Spearfish adopted its current wage scale in the fall of 2025. The scale takes an employee’s job description and level of responsibility into account, assigning each job a grade. Individuals also receive a “step” classification based on their qualifications and tenure.

Condrey and Associates, the human resources firm that prepared the scale, stated that it conducted salary surveys, comparing Spearfish to Aberdeen, Brookings, Box Elder, Mitchell, Sioux Falls, Rapid City, Pierre, Watertown, Yankton, Bismarck, N.D., and Gillette, Wyo. County compensation data for Lawrence, Pennington, and Minnehaha counties were also considered. 

 Chief administrative officer salaries represented in Spearfish wage study 4x4.pdf
*Rapid City and Sioux Falls have neither a city administrator nor a city manager. The salary listed is for those cities’ chief of staff and chief administrative officer, respectively, both are represented in purple. City manager salaries are pictured in yellow. City administrator salaries are in blue. Black Hills Pioneer graphic

Based on the data gathered by the Pioneer, adding in salary data from Gillette and Bismark brought the average pay of leadership positions up virtually across the board. Also, adding in the salaries of city managers, as well as city administrators, brings up the average cost of employing a municipal chief administrative officer. 

At the time of the wage scale’s adoption, Spearfish Human Resources Director Jodi Mathis said that the study placed Spearfish’s then-new, now current, wage scale somewhere in the middle, with higher wage ranges than some cities and lower ranges than others. She said the middle is where the city wants to be.

It’s true that Spearfish is roughly in the middle compared to those communities when it comes to positions like city finance officer, public works director, and chief of police, based on data independently gathered by the Pioneer. 

Spearfish’s finance officer, for example, earns less than the average finance officer salary the communities that Condrey and Associates surveyed, that also responded to the Pioneer. 

Aberdeen and Watertown did not provide salary information by press time. However, the other South Dakota communities surveyed by Condrey and Associates pay their finance officers an average of $ 147,559. Spearfish’s finance officer earns $133,036 annually. 

The wage range for a parks and recreation director in Spearfish — a post that is currently vacant — also aligns with the average salary in surveyed communities. 

Mitchell and Bismark did not provide data for parks and recreation. In the remaining cities, the average salary was $140,200, which falls pretty squarely between $117,582.40 and $178,921.60, the maximum and minimum possible wages for an incoming director under Spearfish’s current wage scale. 

Of the South Dakota cities surveyed by Condrey and Associates, which also responded to the Pioneer, the average city administrator or city manager’s annual wage (in the case of Brookings) is $170,773. Adding in data from Bismark and Gillette, the average salary for those positions is $183,731. That average comes up because Bismark pays $216,212.88 per year, and Gillette pays $229,000.00. 

Reconsidering a city administrator

But the distinction between a city administrator and a city manager is meaningful — something Knox pointed out during the March 11 Spearfish study session. 

Knox offered a presentation on the city’s current form of government — an aldermanic form with a mayor and city council — has options when it comes to retaining a kind of chief administrative officer-type role in the future. The city can operate with a mayor and council alone, hold an election in order to employ a city manager with extended statutory duties, or begin the search for a new city administrator.

“Under that aldermanic form of government, the mayor is considered the CEO of the city and has certain responsibilities in terms of being in charge of faithfully executing and enforcing laws,” Knox said. “Council in that model is really similar to the legislative body in a government — responsible for the purse strings, responsible for approving budgets, approving expenditures, and approving all of the resolutions and the other necessary steps that it takes for the city to conduct business: approving contracts, spending, bills, and the like. That is the outline of where our starting point is.”

A city manager can be hired by the city council, but then that person has greater powers under state law — some of which are currently afforded to Spearfish’s mayor and city council, Knox explained.

“When you go with a city manager, it truly is a change in the form of government,” Knox said. “When you move to city manager, you really are moving that mayor role into more of a weak mayor, and you are pulling away some of the power of the city council as well … we could add to those responsibilities if we wanted to, but we could not take away from them.”

For example, the city council recently voted to remove the sole power to hire and fire staff from the roles and responsibilities of the city administrator, by amending ordinance 1414. 

Under South Dakota State law (SDCL 9.10.13), those powers are defined in descriptions of the city manager role. 

“Except as otherwise provided by law, the manager has power to appoint and remove all officers and employees in the administrative service of the municipality and may authorize the head of any department or office responsible to the manager to appoint and remove subordinates in the department or office. Appointments made by or under the authority of the manager must be made without definite term, on the basis of executive and administrative ability, and on the basis of the training and experience of the appointees in the assigned work,” the law reads. 

With a city administrator, the council has more power to dictate the parameters of the role, Knox said. 

“In this particular form of government, we have the ability as the council and the mayor to shape what those rights and responsibilities are for the city administrator. We can expand them. We can contract them as we see fit,” Knox said.

If the city chooses to hire neither a city administrator nor a city manager, Knox said there might be concerns with continuity. In theory, a city administrator could ensure that city department heads don’t have a new boss every election cycle. However, since the city instituted the city administrator form of government in 2008, turnover has undermined that potential stability.

“Certainly if you take a look at the 9-, even 10- or 11-year period, we’ve had what, 4 city administrators?” Mayor John Senden said.

Knox pointed out that hiring the right candidate could be the key to unlocking longer-term stability. He also offered his opinion on the matter.

“This is just my opinion here, we are a municipality that has got a $53 million budget. In terms of revenue, $58 million in expenses budgeted for this next year. We have 150 to 175 regular employees, when you add seasonal (employees) to that, close to 250 employees providing a wide variety of services,” Knox said. “You need somebody on site, full-time, that can help coordinate various activities, oversee them, keep personnel, keep directors focused moving forward, and working on some of these issues.”

Knox added that he and the mayor had discussed improving the hiring process so that if the city administrator position is maintained, the role can be filled by a candidate who might stick around.

“The mayor and I have discussed … having a thorough review of the process that we have gone through in the past to find out what works, what’s good, and what else we need to add to it to make sure that we are getting the right person for the job, but also the right fit for the community, and the right fit for council,” Knox said.

Senden has said publicly that the city wants to take its time making a decision.

He added that in the past, city administrators have had to go through a tiered interview process, ultimately concluding with an in-person meeting with the city council during a closed-door executive session.

“I think we are going to throw that out the window. When we bring out a candidate, they will have to understand that not only do they go through those step processes, but we are going to have the public come in and have cookies or whatever, so that people can have the opportunity to meet them. That is one of the things we are looking at adding to add community input,” Senden said.

title: Medal of Honor to be awarded to SD man who downed Soviet pilots
byline: Jonathan Ellis The Dakota Scout
description: EDITOR’S NOTE: This Dakota Scout story was published in the Black Hills Pioneer Feb. 10, days after President Donald Trump announced he would present the Medal of Honor to Royce Williams.
_full_text:
EDITOR’S NOTE: This Dakota Scout story was published in the Black Hills Pioneer Feb. 10, days after President Donald Trump announced he would present the Medal of Honor to Royce Williams.

South Dakota native who shot down several Soviet fighter jets will be awarded the Medal of Honor by President Donald Trump.

Royce Williams’ engagement with the Soviets occurred during the Korean War, and to keep tensions between the two superpowers from boiling over, the engagement was kept classified during the Cold War.

Because it was classified, Williams didn’t receive the honor he would have had the dogfight occurred against open combatants, supporters argued. The Medal of Honor is the nation’s highest military award.

Trump called Williams at his home in Escondido, Calif., Wednesday to inform him of the award. Now 100, Williams was born in Wilmot, S.D., and grew up in the area. When he was 11, he moved to the Minnesota side of the Big Stone Lake. While growing up in South Dakota and Minnesota, he enjoyed fishing, hunting and playing sports.

The details on when and where Williams will receive the award have not been finalized.

California Congressman Darrell Issa and South Dakota Sen. Mike Rounds were among the congressional leaders pushing for Williams to get the Medal of Honor.

“Royce Williams is a real-life Top Gun, a true American hero and will now be a recipient of our nation’s highest military award, the Medal of Honor,” Rounds said.

Issa also referred to Williams as a “Top Gun,” the Navy’s best fighter pilots.

“The heroism and valor he demonstrated for more than 35 harrowing minutes almost 70 years ago in the skies over the North Pacific and the coast of North Korea unquestionably saved the lives of his fellow pilots, shipmates, and crew,” he said. “His story is one for the ages and it now has its rightful chapter as Royce receives the Medal of Honor.”

Williams had been given a Silver Star after the hushed engagement that occurred Nov. 18, 1952 during the height of the Korean War. Following the end of the Cold War, Soviet-era military documents entered the public domain. They confirmed the engagement, with at least four Soviet pilots losing their lives in the engagement.

Ben Jones, the director of the South Dakota State Historical Society and a retired Air Force officer, found a declassified U.S. intelligence report based on intercepted communications of Soviet pilots being ordered to attack U.S. Navy ships that were near the border shared by North Korea, China and the Soviet Union.

At the time of the engagement, the U.S. and allied forces were openly fighting communist North Korea, which had invaded South Korea. Communist China joined North Korea when it appeared they might lose the war.

The Soviets were helping North Korea and China clandestinely.

Williams was flying a patrol from the USS Oriskany aircraft carrier with two other U.S. fighter pilots when the engagement occurred. The pilots were warned that MIG-15s were approaching. Soon, Williams found himself all alone.

What followed was an epic dog fight in which Williams, flying an inferior fighter jet to the MIG-15, shot down at least four, and possibly more, Soviet pilots. There were at least seven MIGs in the fight.

The engagement is thought to have been the longest dogfight in U.S. military history, lasting 38 minutes. Williams’ fighter was badly shot up when it managed to limp back to the Oriskany.

Williams figured he was flying against North Korean or Chinese pilots. But after landing, he was summoned by Adm. Robert Briscoe. The admiral told him that the MIG pilots were in fact Soviets. He was warned to keep the engagement secret because they didn’t want to enflame tensions with the Soviets or reveal intelligence capabilities.

Williams kept the secret, not even telling his wife until the engagement was declassified.

He went on to fly combat missions in the Vietnam War. His career in the Navy lasted 37 years, and he flew 227 combat missions. He retired a captain.

A provision inserted into the National Defense Authorization Act allowed the Trump Administration to consider Williams for the Medal of Honor. The act passed in December.

“I’m pleased that President Trump and his team made the determination that Capt. Williams is deserving of this honor and I’m looking forward to celebrating this momentous occasion,” Rounds said.

title: Artemis House encourages community to read  ‘Know My Name’ this April
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Artemis House encourages community to read  ‘Know My Name’ this April
Courtesy photo 

SPEARFISH — April is Sexual Assault Awareness Month, and the Victims of Violence Intervention Program, which operates Artemis House (VVIP) is inviting the community to participate in efforts to raise awareness and prevent sexual violence. Throughout the month, several events will take place, including a community book read.

Artemis House will host a community book read for Chanel Miller’s memoir, “Know My Name,” throughout the month. “This book takes one on a journey through a tragedy, the legal process, and the pathway to healing. It forces us to take a good look at how we as members of society can better support survivors of sexual assault,” said Artemis House Executive Director Renae Servaty.

This initiative will include a month-long discussion group on Facebook (https://www.facebook.com/groups/readwithartemis), allowing participants to share insights and engage with various discussion topics. The kickoff event for the community book read will take place at Henry’s Books in Spearfish at 9 a.m. Tuesday, April 2.

Artemis House partnered with the Spearfish Zonta Club and Henry’s Books Foundation to be able to provide 40 free books.

To encourage discussion and allow for community engagement, a free book discussion will be held at 6:30 p.m. on April 30 at the Matthews Opera House.

Attendees can pose questions to an expert panel and learn ways to support survivors and prevent sexual assault. The expert panel will consist of a law enforcement detective, a prosecuting attorney, a victim specialist, a counselor, and an advocate. Community members are encouraged to provide questions anonymously for the panel via the Artemis House website.

“The crime of sexual assault has been dependent on the silence of its victims. We want to break that silence and let survivors know they are not alone.” Servaty said.

For further information, community members can contact the Artemis House at (605)642-7825 or visit their website at artemishouse.org/awareness.

title: Quick Picks: Easter for kids
description: Easter is just 2 weeks away, and we wanted to give you and your kiddo time to hop into a good holiday-themed book.
_full_text:
Quick Picks: Easter for kids

Easter is just 2 weeks away, and we wanted to give you and your kiddo time to hop into a good holiday-themed book. 

This week’s Easter-inspired children’s books are recommended and described by librarians at Lead’s Hearst Library. 

1) “The Bunnies’ Easter Bonnet “by Nan Roloff

Genre: Picture book 

A group of bunnies prepare a special Easter bonnet decorated with spring flowers and festive touches. Along the way they learn about cooperation and the joy of celebrating Easter together.

2) “Happy Easter” by Kurt Wiese

Genre: Picture book 

Mama Rabbit sends her young bunnies to gather eggs for Easter, but painting them leads to an unexpected and colorful surprise. The playful story celebrates creativity and springtime fun.

3) “Hooray, it’s Easter!” by George McNeill 

Genre: Picture book

Children and animals prepare for Easter by decorating eggs and enjoying the excitement of the holiday. The story captures the joy and traditions of celebrating Easter together.

4) “It’s the Easter Beagle, Charlie Brown” by Charles M. Schulz

Genre: Children’s fiction

The Peanuts gang gets ready for Easter, but the Easter Beagle’s visit turns the day into a mix of excitement and humorous mishaps—especially when Charlie Brown hopes for a better holiday than usual.

5) “The Easter Bunny that Overslept” by Priscilla and Otto Friedrich 

Genre: Picture book 

The Easter Bunny accidentally sleeps through Easter and tries to deliver eggs on other holidays, only to discover they don’t quite fit the occasion. With help from Santa, he gets an alarm clock so he’ll never miss Easter again.

6) “Easter Surprise!” by Anna Jones

Genre: Children’s fiction

As Easter approaches, animals eagerly prepare for the holiday and an egg hunt. The day brings a cheerful surprise that makes the celebration even more special.

title: Governor vetoes home care regulation bill
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE —Gov. Larry Rhoden vetoed a bill on Wednesday that would require state licensing of non-medical home care agencies, his second veto of the 2026 legislative session.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE —Gov. Larry Rhoden vetoed a bill on Wednesday that would require state licensing of non-medical home care agencies, his second veto of the 2026 legislative session.

The vetoed bill is sponsored by Brookings Republican Rep. Mellissa Heermann, who said she introduced the legislation to protect “vulnerable” South Dakotans using personal or home care services — often people who are disabled or elderly. 

South Dakota doesn’t mandate licensure, training or background checks for home services unless they’re paid through Medicaid. The bill would require eight hours of training and background checks for all non-medical home care workers, along with a $100 annual license fee for each non-medical home care agency.

Lawmakers will consider the veto Monday. Overriding it will require a two-thirds vote in each chamber — 47 votes in the House and 24 in the Senate. The bill was supported 41-25 by the state House in February and 20-14 earlier this month in the state Senate. 

Rhoden explained his veto in a letter, saying the regulations in the bill are too broad and create a false sense of consumer protection because the state Department of Health doesn’t have authority to examine or verify the background checks required in the bill. A representative with the state Department of Health opposed the bill during legislative committee hearings.

Rhoden’s explanation surprised Heermann, who said she worked with the Health Department on a compromise. Originally, the bill was “more aggressive” in its protections.

“We made this compromise in good faith, but one of the concerns is that it’s not strong enough now,” Heermann said.

The Department of Health continued to oppose the bill after Heermann’s amendments.

More regulations could risk reducing access to services in rural areas of the state, Rhoden added. He said in a news release that state regulation should be “as limited and targeted as possible.”

“I am directing the Department of Health to work with stakeholders to develop a more effective framework that provides meaningful protections without placing unnecessary burdens on those who provide essential services,” Rhoden said. 

Heermann said if the Legislature doesn’t override Rhoden’s veto, she’ll push for legislative discussions with the executive branch, stakeholders and affected South Dakotans to introduce a bill next winter if she’s reelected.

“I think it’s a critical piece of legislation and a critical protection that should already be in place,” Heermann said. “When it comes to veto day, I think it’s a heavy lift and involves many conversations.”

Rhoden has signed 222 bills into law and vetoed two this legislative session. The other veto is on a bill that would ban lab-grown meat in the state. Although lawmakers have not yet considered that veto, Rhoden has signed a compromise bill into law that will place a 5-year moratorium on lab-grown meat.

title: Governor’s approval opens  SD housing loan fund to  big-city airport projects
byline: By Seth Tupper Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: RAPID CITY — A state loan fund intended to incentivize housing construction will soon be available for airport improvement and maintenance projects in Sioux Falls and Rapid City.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

RAPID CITY — A state loan fund intended to incentivize housing construction will soon be available for airport improvement and maintenance projects in Sioux Falls and Rapid City.

The change is in legislation that lawmakers approved earlier this month and South Dakota Republican Gov. Larry Rhoden signed into law Tuesday while visiting the Rapid City Regional Airport.

“More gates, more flights and more destinations means more visitors, more business and more opportunity,” Rhoden said.

The money for the loans is held by the state’s Housing Infrastructure Financing Program. Legislators and then-Gov. Kristi Noem created the program three years ago. The money can be used for housing-related infrastructure such as streets, roads, sewer lines and water lines.

The fund has supported 85 projects across the state, helping create 7,421 single-family housing lots or units and 5,754 apartment or other multi-family units.

The program initially held a $200 million mix of state and federal funds. The state Housing Authority issued half of the money as grants. The other half, for loans, had about $65 million remaining as of late last year, which led the governor and lawmakers to propose tapping into the fund for airport projects.

The new law will take effect in July. It allows for loans from the fund to South Dakota airports in metropolitan statistical areas with populations of at least 125,000, which is a threshold that only the Sioux Falls and Rapid City areas currently meet. The loans will have 20-year terms at 2% annual interest.

Last year, lawmakers and Rhoden authorized a $15 million, zero-interest loan from the housing fund to help with construction of a new elementary school in the Douglas School District, based in Box Elder. The district, which borders Ellsworth Air Force Base, is experiencing rapid growth related to the impending addition of B-21 bomber planes.

This year’s bill is one of 210 that Rhoden has signed into law from the annual legislative session. Another bill Rhoden signed Tuesday will add “necessary site work” to the list of allowable uses for housing infrastructure money, and change the program’s maximum loans from one-third to one-half of total project costs.

Lawmakers concluded most of their work March 12 but will return Monday to the Capitol in Pierre to consider the governor’s vetoes. He has issued one veto so far against a ban on lab-grown meat, as part of his compromise support of a five-year moratorium that he approved.

title: State Historic Preservation Office awards Deadwood Fund Grant to 8 communities
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: PIERRE — The South Dakota State Historical Society has Deadwood Fund Grant monies to 8 communities throughout the state for historic preservation projects for the spring cycle of funding.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

PIERRE — The South Dakota State Historical Society has Deadwood Fund Grant monies to 8 communities throughout the state for historic preservation projects for the spring cycle of funding. 

Deadwood Fund program is funded exclusively by a portion of gaming revenue for the administration of historic preservation projects, as required by state law, to promote the rehabilitation and restoration of historic properties across South Dakota. 

“For the past 30 years, this program has served as a great way to encourage restoration or rehabilitation of historic properties across our state,” said Garry Guan, state historic preservation officer. “It is one more way we can promote and preserve our historic and cultural heritage of South Dakota.”

The projects receiving matching funds are as follows:

• Fort Sisseton State Park office quarters, carpenters’ shop, and blacksmith shop located in Lake City, received $15,000 to assist in window preservation. 

• The Immanuel Lutheran Church, located in Zeona and built in 1923, received $5,000 for repairs to the steeple and roof. 

• The Saint Wenceslaus Church Rectory, located in Tabor, received $10,000 for repairs to the porch. 

• Camp Bob Marshall Cabins, located in Custer, received $15,000 for a roofing repair project. 

• Canton Lutheran Church, located in Canton, received $15,000 for repairs to stained glass. 

• 123 North Main St. in Mitchell received $15,000 for structural work. 

• Hyden House, located in Alcester, received $10,000 for repairs to the roof. 

• Smith House, located in Madison, received $10,000 for repairs to the roof. 

The Deadwood Fund Grant program awards projects annually with 2 application deadlines — Feb. 1 and Oct. 1. For more information on the Deadwood Fund Grant program, contact the State Historic Preservation Office at (605) 773-6000.

title: SD Mines invites community to experience a world of culture
byline: Pioneer Staff Reports
description: RAPID CITY — For more than 6 decades, South Dakota Mines international students have shared a little of their home countries with the community during the annual Cultural Expo, an event that brings people together to celebrate global cultures.
_full_text:
RAPID CITY — For more than 6 decades, South Dakota Mines international students have shared a little of their home countries with the community during the annual Cultural Expo, an event that brings people together to celebrate global cultures.

The event, which began 64 years ago with the university’s first international students, has grown immensely over the years and has become a must-attend tradition.

“South Dakota Mines’ Cultural Expo is a place for global exchange, curiosity, community, and learning from different cultures — values that align well with STEM fields,” said Beth Riley, director of the university’s Ivanhoe International Center. “As the world becomes a global society, engaging with different cultures expands professional and personal perspectives, sparks curiosity and intellectual exploration beyond the classroom environment, and is a wonderful event celebrating many cultures together.”

The 64th Annual Cultural Expo will be held from 11 a.m. to 1:30 p.m. Saturday in the Surbeck Center’s Beck Ballroom. There will be food tasting, entertainment and country displays. The cost is $15 per person, $5 for children 10 and under, and provides participants with a set of sampling tickets to be used at various booths.  

The doors open at 11 with a lineup of free entertainment featuring traditional music, vibrant dance and a world-class fashion show. Food sampling beings at 11:30 and allows participants to enjoy a variety of authentic international foods while the performances continue on stage.

Five countries and 2 organizations will be represented at this year’s event.

Signature events like the Cultural Expo connect communities, celebrate traditions, strengthen community bonds and educate younger generations about diverse cultures.

title: Q&A with author Dan McKittrick
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
The Tipperary Tale.jpg
Courtesy photo 

 Dan Portrait.jpg
Courtesy photo 

Former Sioux Falls English teacher Dan McKittrick’s writing journey started during the COVID-19 pandemic. 

“I had always wanted to write, and I thought this was a beautiful opportunity. Plus, I thought I might die, and now was the best time to do it,” McKittrick said.

He has since written and self-published 6 novels. 

McKittrick visited with the Pioneer about his latest book, “Tipperary Tale,” which published on March 8. 

Q: Describe your new book.

A: “The Tipperary Tale” is a novel about a 17 year-old high school senior in America in 2026. Tommy Ryan finds his country bitterly divided, chaotic, and cruel.  It has been that way his entire life and he is fed up.  Instead of going off to college like his classmates, he decides to move to Ireland. His family’s ancestors are from there and he figures it will be more safe and more sane. It is a coming of age story about one young man who is trying to make sense of life in present day America.

Q: What inspired you to write this particular story?

A: I wondered what the current situation with our politics and social discourse must feel like for young people.  When I was a kid we had people like John and Robert Kennedy, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., and other leaders who inspired us rather than tearing us down and apart.  We had serious challenges then too, like the disagreement over the Vietnam War and racial segregation, but we had good leaders and could still talk civilly to each other. Congress still took their responsibilities seriously.  I feel sorry for young people whose only frame of reference is how their country is now. It has to be harder than ever to grow up in today’s world.

Q: What makes a good book/story in your genre?

A: One that is realistic and speaks with an authentic voice.  In “Tipperary Tale” the main character is angry and confused.  He has been led to believe all the adults in his world are crazy.  They are supposed to be good role models and examples for young people but they all seem to be stressed out, angry, and confused, too.

I think readers want to be able to put themselves into the story and feel what the character feels.  They don’t want the author to sugar coat it.  In Tommy’s case, desperate times call for desperate measures.

Q: What do you hope reader take away from your new book?

A: I hope they take away the perspective that the current times we are living through leave an impression on our young people.  We expect them to move confidently into the future, but they are looking around at the adults in their lives who are not at all confident or at peace about the future.  Those adults are as confused as the young people.  But perseverance and redemption are still possible.

Q: Which authors/books have inspired you?

A: I thought of “Catcher in the Rye” as I wrote this book.  J.D. Sallinger captured the disorientation and frustration and anger of being a kid in that story and expressed beautifully the angst and the humor of his main character, Holden Caulfield.  I also appreciate the work of Kurt Vonnegut and Tom Robbins.  Also, a book I’ve revisited a few times over the years is “Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance.”

title: Plaque unveiled at SD Capitol for  100-year-old Medal of Honor recipient
byline: By Meghan O’Brien Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: EDITOR’S NOTE: This Dakota Scout story was published in the Black Hills Pioneer Feb. 10, days after President Donald Trump announced he would present the Medal of Honor to Royce Williams.
_full_text:
Medal of Honor to be awarded to SD man who downed Soviet pilots
EDITOR’S NOTE: This Dakota Scout story was published in the Black Hills Pioneer Feb. 10, days after President Donald Trump announced he would present the Medal of Honor to Royce Williams. 

 Plaque unveiled at South Dakota Capitol for 100-year-old Medal of Honor recipient.jpg
Gov. Larry Rhoden, left, and Lt. Gov. Tony Venhuizen unveil a plaque for retired U.S. Navy Capt. E. Royce Williams in the Hall of Honor at the Capitol in Pierre Wednesday. Photo by Meghan O’Brien, South Dakota Searchlight 

 State of the Union
Retired Navy Capt. E. Royce Williams acknowledges applause after receiving the Medal of Honor during President Donald Trump’s State of the Union address at the U.S. Capitol on Feb. 24, 2026, in Washington, D.C. AP photo 

PIERRE — There’s a new name in the South Dakota Hall of Honor at the state Capitol building.

One-hundred-year-old South Dakota native and retired U.S. Navy Capt. E. Royce Williams was celebrated at a Wednesday ceremony where a plaque honoring him was unveiled, although Williams did not attend.

“In spite of being outnumbered and facing incredible danger, Captain Williams engaged the enemy with courage and skill,” said Republican Gov. Larry Rhoden. “Our state has always had a strong tradition of service, and Captain Williams is the very best of that tradition.”

President Donald Trump awarded Williams the Medal of Honor, the country’s highest military honor, at the State of the Union address earlier this year. The medal honors actions by Williams that had been classified for decades.

“His story was secret for over 50 years, he didn’t even want to tell his wife, but the legend grew and grew,” Trump said during the speech in February. “But tonight, at 100 years old, this brave Navy captain is finally getting the recognition he deserves.”

On Nov. 18, 1952, over Korean coastal waters during the Korean War, then-Lt. Williams, from Wilmot, South Dakota, led 3 F9F Panthers against seven Soviet MiG-15s. He disabled 3 enemy jets and damaged a fourth.

The Soviet jets, according to the U.S. Naval Institute, were “superior to the F9F in almost every fashion.” The mission was the only direct overwater combat between U.S. Navy fighters and Soviet fighters during the Cold War.

Williams, one of 11 Medal of Honor recipients from South Dakota, now lives in California. The Hall of Honor at the South Dakota Capitol is located in the hallway that visitors enter immediately after going through security.

title: Work continues to restore, renovate opera house atrium, art gallery, more
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: Significant improvements have been underway at the Historic Homestake Opera House this week. Lead Main Street was blocked off to one lane of traffic for a few hours Wednesday morning, while crews from One Way Service Pros worked to lift 800-pound HVAC units on to the roof of the Historic Homestake Opera House. The new HVAC system will significantly improve heating and cooling in the mercantile section of the opera house building. Pioneer photo by Wendy Pitlick
_full_text:
HVAC 2.jpeg
Significant improvements have been underway at the Historic Homestake Opera House this week. Lead Main Street was blocked off to one lane of traffic for a few hours Wednesday morning, while crews from One Way Service Pros worked to lift 800-pound HVAC units on to the roof of the Historic Homestake Opera House. The new HVAC system will significantly improve heating and cooling in the mercantile section of the opera house building. Pioneer photo by Wendy Pitlick 

 museum glass.jpeg
Historic Homestake Opera House Executive Director Todd Jones shows off the museum-quality glass that now covers part of the burn area in the opera house theater. The glass is intended to preserve a burned section of the walls as a display, so patrons can see what it looked like after the 1984 fire, and how restoration efforts have brought it back to its original grandeur. Pioneer photo by Wendy Pitlick 

LEAD — Strategic projects that will allow audiences to comfortably use the entire Historic Homestake Opera House for several different kinds of performing and visual arts, while restoring the building to its original grandeur, are well underway. 

In 2025 and early 2026, visible progress to replace windows in the atrium of the opera house, and start work to install electricity in the mercantile part of the opera house for a pottery and framing workshop area was completed. The work will allow usable space for a kiln that was donated to the Lead-Deadwood Arts Center in 2021, as well as greatly improve comfort and usability throughout the building. 

“For us to finally get that up and get it online is really important,” said Todd Jones, executive director of the Historic Homestake Opera House. “It becomes another way to get people in the building, get people utilizing the space, and it lets us lean into some educational pieces related to art. Getting this done, to me, really is key to being able to do that next part.” 

In early 2026, work started to install the new HVAC system in the former library space of the opera house, with a “doghouse” installed on the roof. The “doghouse” will allow personnel to easily access the roof for snow shoveling, and utility maintenance, and repair. The work to heat and improve ventilation in that section of the building will greatly improve its usability, when work starts to restore the theater, Jones said. 

“Getting the HVAC and everything online for this section of the building, which we refer to as the mercantile, allows us to have spaces available when we are not able to access the theater space,” Jones said. “It allows us to continue offering programming and keep our doors open, keep things moving forward, when for most theaters that would be an impossibility. So to get this done really is strategic at this point.” 

Jones said public use in the atrium and library area is already starting to ramp up, as audiences get used to using the alternative space. That’s important for when the theater is not available during renovations, because it will allow programming to continue at the opera house, Jones said. In 2025 it was used for community events and music programs, and in 2026 it will be used for some small-scale shows, providing a unique experience for audiences. 

“You don’t often get theater in the round,” Jones said. “You don’t often get theater as intimate and up close as you have up here. So those are really good opportunities for us to introduce the community to new expressions of performing arts, but also introduce them to this space in a different way. It’s really becoming an important and versatile space for us in this building, helping to keep the doors open and keep us viable.” 

Using the entire building to promote the arts while restoration continues is also the reason for renovating the Lead-Deadwood Arts Center space, Jones said. In February, crews began demolition and construction work to reconfigure the arts center space to host both the opera house concession area and the art gallery. Currently the concession area is located in the coat closet of the lobby, and the plan is to move it into the arts center, to allow more space for snacks and goodies, to reduce traffic congestion through the lobby, and to give artists more exposure, as patrons have the chance to peruse their work at the arts center while attending shows. 

“As anyone who has ever worked concessions there knows, it’s a very tight space,” he said. “The other issue with our concessions is it causes a log jam in the middle of the lobby. You’ve got people ticketing and trying to get through while there is a huge line at concessions, blocking that section fo the lobby. So, if we can re-orient where our concessions are, we can move people through a lot more smoothly, a lot more quickly, and it gives them the opportunity to really explore another portion of our building. So they can explore the art gallery whiel they’re getting their concessions.” 

All of this work, Jones said, positions the opera house to focus on heavy restoration work on the theater, while encouraging use for the rest of the building. In 2026, Jones said the Historic Homestake Opera House plans to start another capital campaign to raise $13-$16 million, to pay for new electrical and mechanical systems in the theater, including new lights and sound that will be designed to serve shows for the next 30-50 years. It will pay for plaster work and other finishing touches, exterior work on the outside of the building, and new, energy efficient windows in the lobby, and replace more than 250 ballasts in the opera house with LED lighting. 

“We really need to address that too and get those buttoned up and where they need to be, so we’re not just putting in a new boiler system to hemorrhage all the heat outdoors,” Jones said. “Ballasts take an incredible amount of energy. So if we can eliminate those and get to LEDs, we could drop our energy usage significantly just on lighting the building. We’re really trying to push into that and make that part of the project.” 

A successful 2026 capital campaign will also include money to add to an existing endowment at the opera house, which will pay for ongoing maintenance and repairs, as well as allocate dedicated funds for education programming and internships for students at Black Hills State University, for the theater and the arts center. 

“That’s not money we want to use for paying the electric bill. That’s money we want to use if we need to replace a window, if we had cracks in the plasterwork, and we’d want to be able to address that,” Jones said of the endowment. “The other thing that would allow us to do is internships. If there are students that want to learn more about front of house, more about directing, and want to get different experiences on the stage, hopefully we’ll be able to build into those relationships. There is also an art gallery in the building, so to be able to do it there, too. If there are students who are interested in learning how do you run an art gallery, or who would like to teach art. We’ve got this kiln, and it might be an opportunity if you want to lean into that a little bit. That takes funding that our programming and operations might not be able to handle, but a healthy endowment will allow us to do that.” 

The ultimate goal, Jones said, has been to proceed with full speed ahead, on the restoration project that has dominated the opera house for decades. 

“We want to be positioned in such a way that what we’re asking of the community is to come in and enjoy the building, not to come in and finance the building,” he said. “That’s really where we want to be when we’re all done, is to be able to look out and go the building is finished and it’s back where it needs to be. It looks like it did when it opened in 1914, but it’s got technology for 2050 and beyond, and we’re positioned well with an endowment that allows us to keep this building maintained into perpetuity. If we can get there, then this really does become a gift back to Lead and the Northern Black Hills.”

title: 17th performance of Easter Cantata  to feature ‘Upon This Rock’
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: The 63-member choir rehearses for the upcoming Easter Cantata, which will be performed on Palm Sunday, March 29, and Tuesday, March 31, at 7 p.m. at Grace Lutheran Church in Sturgis. Pioneer photo by Tim Potts
_full_text:
17th performance of Easter Cantata  to feature ‘Upon This Rock’
The 63-member choir rehearses for the upcoming Easter Cantata, which will be performed on Palm Sunday, March 29, and Tuesday, March 31, at 7 p.m. at Grace Lutheran Church in Sturgis. Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

STURGIS — Area singers will once again share the Easter message through music as the community Easter Cantata returns for its 17th performance with this year’s presentation of ‘Upon This Rock.’  The cantata will be performed on Palm Sunday, March 29, and again on Tuesday, March 31, with both performances beginning at 7 p.m. at Grace Lutheran Church, 1061 Sherman St., in Sturgis.  A 63-member community choir began rehearsals Feb. 15 in preparation for the performances.

This year’s cantata, composed by Pepper Choplin, tells the Easter story through the voice and perspective of Peter, one of Jesus’ closest disciples. Through narration and music, the work follows Peter’s journey of faith, doubt and redemption.

“This first-person accounting of the Easter message is told by Peter,” said director Marlene Kotab. “It’s a more personal telling of the story from Peter’s perspective, a man who was always with Jesus but had the same doubts and frailties that we all experience.”

As the story unfolds through Peter’s experiences with Christ, the cantata invites listeners to reflect on their own lives and faith, asking questions such as what their passion and calling might be.

The production features narration by Randy Bender in the role of Peter.  Preparing a large community choir for the cantata has changed over the years, Kotab said. While earlier productions often relied on full group rehearsals, today’s busy schedules mean singers must take more responsibility for practicing on their own.

“Times have changed since I first started organizing the cantata in 2007,” Kotab said. “People are more mobile and busier now. So I rely on the singers’ at-home practice. I rehearse with whoever comes on Sunday afternoons and pray for the best outcome, and it’s always fantastic.”

Despite the logistical challenges, Kotab said the cantata continues to be a meaningful tradition for both the performers and the audience.

“It is the faith of the people who sing,” she said. “They want to share the message of Easter, and they love to sing about it. Plus, they don’t have many opportunities to sing in a choir this large. There’s nothing like the power and emotion of singing with other good singers. It’s truly a shared gift.”

Community members are invited to attend the performances and experience the Easter story through music and narration. There is no admission charge, although a free-will offering will be accepted to help cover expenses.

title: Gold standard  leaders in Lead recognized at annual chamber banquet
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: LEAD — In 2025, the Lead Area Chamber of Commerce hosted 2 new events while facing new financial hurdles. In reflecting on the year’s challenges and achievements, Chamber Board President Levi Wilson said the organization continued to drive engagement in a community that consistently “punches above its weight class” when it comes to community events.
_full_text:
LEAD — In 2025, the Lead Area Chamber of Commerce hosted 2 new events while facing new financial hurdles. In reflecting on the year’s challenges and achievements, Chamber Board President Levi Wilson said the organization continued to drive engagement in a community that consistently “punches above its weight class” when it comes to community events.

This year’s chamber banquet took place Wednesday at the historic Homestake Opera House, which Wilson said was a fitting setting for reflecting on the year.

“We decided to move in and partner more closely with the opera house, as you can see. This was a multi-layer decision. One, it reduced our overhead, so it is a fiscally responsible decision. Two, it gave us greater access and the ability to use a frankly amazing facility that we have here and use it in a mutually beneficial manner,” Wilson said. “There is a lot of synergy that can happen between the opera house and Lead Area Chamber of Commerce.”

Both the chamber and the opera house faced some unexpected funding hurdles in 2025.

“The national administration took what I am going to call a wrecking ball to the national grant ecosystem: sources that would have been sources of funding for this event or to offset the cost of other events evaporated literally overnight,” Wilson said. “The South Dakota Arts Council laid off all of their staff except for one in the span of a week. That was a source of funding that evaporated overnight for the chamber, and I know it had an even deeper impact on the opera house.”

The chamber laid off staff in order to balance its budget, and in 2025, it stopped pre-purchasing fireworks.

Fireworks, for Gold Camp Jubilee and Winter Fest, are the chamber’s largest annual expense.

Tariffs, Wilson said, have caused the price of fireworks to shoot up by 30%.

“We are usually insulated from these kinds of things, but national decisions are having negative reverberating local impacts,” Wilson said. “All of that is to say, the chamber is trying to walk the line between being financially responsible while also trying to calibrate.”

This year, the chamber’s goal is to reach a place where it can rehire a staffer and pre-purchase fireworks for events.

For 2026, the chamber has collaborated with the city of Deadwood and Deadwood Historic Preservation, which agreed to contribute $10,000 to sponsor fireworks in Lead.

Last year, the chamber launched Lead Ladles — a fundraiser specifically for fireworks.

“By leaning into the charitable nature of our community chefs, who frankly are weirdly competitive when it comes to soups, we pulled off a successful event that pulled in a whole host of people who haven’t attended any chamber events recently,” Wilson said.

Also new to the community is Lead Con, taking place this year on April 11, an event Wilson described as having been organized by a “council of nerds.”

“Lead Con is, think Comic Con, but shrunk down to the size of Lead,” Wilson said.  “I’m going to say on a pragmatic level, it has very good potential for generating sales tax receipts for the city and has very good potential for generating revenue, foot traffic, and bigger audiences for the Opera House. It also has great potential for being a major cultural and community asset.”

Wilson said he plans to attend in wizard robes and volunteer to usher beginners through Dungeons and Dragons “one shots.” The lead presenter will be Napoleon Dynamite producer Sean Covel, Wilson said.

“Because of that grassroots development and the quirky, niche element of the event, something has happened that I’ve never seen in Lead before: a whole new set of people started stepping up and engaging and volunteering. We managed to get new people to volunteer for an event in Lead,” Wilson said.

During the awards portion of the chamber banquet, volunteers have a moment in the spotlight.

Volunteer of the Year: Christine Allen

This year, the chamber honored one outstanding volunteer in particular — Christine Allen. Lead Chamber Executive Director Jami Grangaard described Allen as the kind of person who will drop everything to help a neighbor in need.

Allen received the Miles Beyond Ordinary Award for Volunteer of the Year, an honor awarded to “an individual who demonstrated exceptional dedication, generosity of time, meaningful impact through volunteer service, benefitting the chamber and the broader community.”

Chamber Champion: Todd Jones

The 2025 Lead Chamber Champion is Homestake Opera House Executive Director Todd Jones. Jones, in partnering with the chamber, has advocated for the success of both the historic venue and the non-profit aimed at growing and developing local economic success.

“Chamber Champion honors an individual, business, or organization that goes above and beyond advocating for the Chamber’s mission, actively supporting its members’ programs and vision,” Grangaard said.

Lead the Way, Chamber Business of the Year: Lewies Burgers and Brews

Jeremy McKay took over Lewie’s Burgers and Brews when Lewie Sternhagen passed the torch after more than 30 years in business. McKay has leveraged the business to raise funds for local causes — bringing in $5,000 for the Boys and Girls Club by hosting regular BINGO nights. His goal is to raise $10,000 for the charity this spring.

title: What a season!  BHSU falls at Elite 8
byline: By Jason Gross Black Hills Pioneer
description: PITTSBURGH, Pa. — Black Hills State University’s postseason run ended Wednesday as the Yellow Jackets fell 76-66 decision to Nova Southeastern in an NCAA Division II Elite 8 men’s basketball tournament contest.
_full_text:
PITTSBURGH, Pa. — Black Hills State University’s postseason run ended Wednesday as the Yellow Jackets fell 76-66 decision to Nova Southeastern in an NCAA Division II Elite 8 men’s basketball tournament contest.

“We created a lot of good looks; we just didn’t capitalize on them,” Yellow Jackets’ head coach Ryan Thompson said. He added Black Hills State missed a lot of open shots, and some decision making was not as good as it needed to be.

Thompson emphasized that top-seeded and defending national champion Nova Southeastern had something to do with the Yellow Jackets’ struggles. He added the Sharks’ pressure played a significant role.

Nova Southeastern led 22-15 in the first half. BJ Woodruff connected on a pair of field goals — 1 from 3-point range — to bring the 8th-seeded Yellow Jackets within 22-20.

Jackson Seale and Cam Lowe added field goals as Black Hills State forged a 24-24 tie with 8 minutes 45 seconds remaining in the first half.

Ross Reeves and Jaxon Nap made 2 free throws apiece to put Nova Southeastern ahead 28-24. Bennett Kayser’s 3-point field goal, plus 4 more points from Reeves, helped the Sharks extend their advantage to 35-24.

Jack Haggett’s 2 free throws ended a nearly 7-minute scoring drought and brought the Yellow Jackets within 36-26. Woodruff connected from 3-point range as Black Hills State cut the margin to 36-29.

Nova Southeastern led 40-29 at halftime, with the Yellow Jackets missing 13 of their last 14 field goal attempts during the first 20 minutes. 

The Sharks led 44-31 early in the second half. Myles Johnson and Lowe keyed an 8-0 run midway through the 2nd half as Black Hills State sliced its deficit to 52-50.

Nova Southeastern responded with a 9-0 run. Kayser’s 3-point field goal gave the Sharks a 61-50 edge.

Blake Volmer’s 3-point field goal with 4 1/2 minutes remaining kept the Yellow Jackets within 67-60. They got no closer than 6 points the rest of the way.

Woodruff scored 19 points to pace Black Hills State. Five of his field goals were from 3-point range.

Joel Speckman and Jaeton Hackley added 10 points apiece for the Yellow Jackets. They ended 30-5, which is a school record for wins in a season. 

Nova Southeastern received double-figure scoring from 4 players, led by Reeves’ 31 points. Nap (13 points), Dallas Graziani (12), and Kayser (11) followed.

Thompson said the outcome was disappointing and added he felt like this group was so connected and had such a great chemistry. He referenced the Yellow Jackets’ Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference regular-season and tournament titles, plus the South Central Regional crown.

“We had a group of guys that did things the right way,” Thompson said. “I’m so proud of the way in which our guys carried themselves all season.”

Thompson said Black Hills State endured foul trouble and a poor shooting night, and was not its best in some ways. He praised Hackley and Woodruff for their efforts.

“It’s an honor, and it’s a blessing,” Hackley said about being part of the program and helping lead the Yellow Jackets to this stage. “I definitely don’t take this for granted.”

Hackley said he plays his hardest and does his best to give the team the best chance of winning. 

“We came up short tonight; that definitely hurts,” Hackley said. He added he is really proud of the team and its play this season.

Woodruff said he is really grateful for this journey and his teammates. “I just try to stay present, and it definitely made me hungry for more,” he added.

Black Hills State scoring: BJ Woodruff 19, Jaeton Hackley 10, Joel Speckman 10, Myles Johnson 7, Tristan Hurdle 6, Cam Lowe 5, Jackson Seale 4, Blake Volmer 3, Jack Haggett 2. Totals 24 field goals, 8 of 11 from the free throw line, 66 points.

Nova Southeastern scoring: Ross Reeves 31, Jaxon Nap 13, Dallas Graziani 12, Bennett Kayser 11, Bobby Rosenberger 6, Ryan Davis 3. Totals 24 field goals, 23 of 34 from the free throw line, 76 points.

Field goal percentages: Black Hills State 38 (24-63), Nova Southeastern 36 (24-65)

Three-point field goals: Black Hills State 10 (Woodruff 5, Hurdle 2, Johnson 1, Lowe 1, Volmer 1), Nova Southeastern 5 (Kayser 2, Nap 1, Reeves 1, Rosenberger 1)

Rebounds: Black Hills State 41 (Haggett 11), Nova Southeastern 46 (Davis 13)

Turnovers: Black Hills State 14, Nova Southeastern 7

Total fouls: Black Hills State 23, Nova Southeastern 14

Third-seeded Gannon toppled number 6 seed Michigan Tech 75-49 in Wednesday’s first game. 

Oklahoma Baptist entered Wednesday as the number 7 seed and stunned second-seeded Cal State East Bay 78-55. 

Gannon and Oklahoma Baptist will meet in a Final 4 game on Friday.

Lander, the number 5 seed, held off fourth-seeded Daemen in Wednesday’s final game and will face Nova Southeastern in Friday’s other Final 4 contest.
